206955.explanation: The Christian Union prioritizes support and assistance for our children, young people, and families. The Christian Union aims to work towards a system in which the perspective of the young person and their environment is central.

206956.explanation: Through all available services, we want to help people with care, financial support, and basic necessities such as shelter, hygiene, and food, so that people do not have to resort to begging. If begging does occur and leads to public nuisance, enforcement officers may intervene (proportionate).

206957.explanation: Funds allocated for climate policy should remain dedicated to climate policy. Because it is important to leave our planet in good condition for our children, these funds should be spent for that purpose.

206958.explanation: Working completely fossil-free is not yet realistic. We are working towards a fossil-free generation. In doing so, we take responsibility while balancing economic development and sustainability. In this way, future generations can also benefit from a thriving business climate.

206959.explanation: It is not a tax on having a terrace, but a tax on using public space for a hospitality business. There should be a fair contribution in return for the use of public space.

206960.explanation: We invest heavily in making public space accessible and attractive, and therefore levy a tax on its use. Many other cities have preceded us with positive results.

206961.explanation: We are attentive to residents and neighborhoods that need support in particular. We believe it is important to make additional investments in neighborhoods that are struggling, while also having a responsibility for care and livability for all our residents.

206962.explanation: We do not support a 24/7 economy in which everything must always be possible. Having a collective moment of rest is also beneficial for society. It also helps protect small businesses from competitive pressure from large chains.

206963.explanation: We ensure dignified living conditions. Vulnerability should never lead to invisibility. As the Christian Union, we look out for our neighbors and provide shelter to those who need it. We want no one to be forced to sleep on the streets or under bridges.

206964.explanation: Without priority, refugees would have to remain in asylum centers for years. They make up only a small proportion of people seeking housing. Refugees with a residence permit are also residents of our municipality.

206965.explanation: We ensure dignified living conditions. Vulnerability should never lead to invisibility. As the Christian Union, we care for our neighbors and provide shelter to those who need it. We want no one to be forced to sleep on the streets or under bridges.

206966.explanation: If employers want to offer work to low-paid labor migrants, it is also their responsibility to provide suitable housing nearby.

206967.explanation: Refugees in asylum centers fall under the responsibility of the COA. Once they receive a residence permit, they become eligible for social services and, where applicable, for the "Stadjerspas" as well.

206968.explanation: We expect companies to contribute to sustainability, but it goes too far to make this an absolute requirement.

206969.explanation: We assume that fishermen handle fish properly and responsibly and comply with existing regulations. As stewards, we are called to take good care of creation.

206970.explanation: (Heated) terraces are part of Groningen’s vibrant atmosphere, as long as that terrace heating is offered in a sustainable way.

206971.explanation: In locations where it is necessary for safety. In other places, we choose not to do so in order to protect biodiversity.

206972.explanation: As a municipality, we do not force people to move away from gas. However, the energy transition is necessary, and we support people in this process.

206973.explanation: WarmteStad provides residents with affordable heat. It is good for the municipality to retain control over this, rather than leaving it to the market, in order to take good care of our residents.

206974.explanation: Our preference is for rooftops, but if necessary, the option of using agricultural land should be available.

206975.explanation: Green space is not an extra, but essential for health and well-being. Green areas bring people together and encourage social interaction. Investing in green space is therefore also an investment in social cohesion and livability. We plead for abundant green space in our neighborhoods and villages

206976.explanation: This is possible if events are well distributed across the municipality.

206977.explanation: Organizers can certainly be asked to contribute to sustainability, but we do not make this a mandatory condition.

206978.explanation: Many women experience inappropriate or abusive behavior. It is important that there is a place where they can turn for support.

206979.explanation: Festivals are part of our city, and many people enjoy them. We already limit the number of festivals through designated noise days and end times. Further restrictions are not necessary.

206980.explanation: Although we are very cautious with regard to drug use, it is a fact that drugs are present. In that case, we want conditions to be as safe as possible.

206981.explanation: We consider it very important that children are able to participate in sports. For residents who cannot afford this, the Youth Fund for Sports and Culture is available. We support a compassionate poverty policy and direct our resources toward residents who need this support.

206982.explanation: We already do this through our street-naming policy. Not everything needs to be named after these resistance heroes.

206983.explanation: Yes, this recognition is important. We ensure meaningful and connecting encounters around commemorations and celebrations. Promoting dialogue and respect for different perspectives is essential for an inclusive society.

206984.explanation: Carbide shooting and Easter fires are cultural and social events. Provided they are carried out safely, the municipality can allow them.

206986.explanation: Because there are already various opportunities for children to become acquainted with art and culture. For residents who cannot afford this, the Youth Fund for Sports and Culture is available.

206987.explanation: FC Groningen is important to our residents. The club is part of Groningen, and preserving its identity is important. We do not want a foreign financier to take over the club; instead, we would prefer supporters to become co-owners.

206988.explanation: Groningen is a city that attracts visitors through art, culture, and music, which generates both pride and economic benefits for entrepreneurs and the hospitality sector. That is why we support a pop music venue at this central location.

206989.explanation: Municipal enforcement officers are being given an increasing number of tasks. Their equipment should be appropriate to these responsibilities. If the minister allows it, we can consider this.

206990.explanation: An expression of faith is not a sign that someone would lack independence. We support diversity within the team.

206991.explanation: The Christian Union wants to focus primarily on prevention and guidance. Municipal enforcement officers play an important role, and we are also deploying additional officers. We will first assess what this increased deployment delivers.

206992.explanation: The condition is that, as part of an integrated plan, it must be well substantiated why this is necessary. Camera surveillance can be a tool, but not a standard solution.

206993.explanation: Applying priority rules is a far-reaching instrument. Giving priority to a specific group hinders others in their search for suitable housing.

206994.explanation: These are important professions that are under pressure. Applying priority rules is a far-reaching instrument. Giving priority to a specific group hinders others in their search for suitable housing.

206995.explanation: Everyone deserves a home. We are working on affordable and sustainable housing and an attractive living environment. This must, however, fit in with the surrounding area.

206996.explanation: Because of the housing shortage, we believe it is important that residents who need a home also have the opportunity to buy one.

206997.explanation: The housing shortage in the Netherlands is severe. If an owner leaves a property vacant without justification, the municipality should be able to intervene.

206998.explanation: Planning and design regulations exist to ensure that buildings fit their surroundings. We do not want to abolish these rules, but we are willing to consider whether fewer regulations could suffice.

206999.explanation: In principle, we opt for mixed neighborhoods, where young and old, rich and poor, people with and without disabilities live together. We also pay attention to student housing, as students are an essential part of our Groningen community.

207000.explanation: Only when infill development is not possible or insufficient for the future and livability of the village. This will be done in close consultation with the immediate surroundings.

207002.explanation: We support mixed neighborhoods and communities.

207003.explanation: The municipality provides tailored support to residents receiving social assistance. This focuses on an appropriate pathway, which may include daytime activities, volunteer work, or paid employment.

207004.explanation: The basic job is an excellent way to enable residents to participate in society. The work they do is of social value.

207006.explanation: This is already part of the Participation Act, although there may be various valid reasons why working is not yet possible.

207007.explanation: We support a compassionate poverty policy. Assistance for children from low-income families is already provided through special social assistance, the Leergeld Foundation, and additional funds.

207008.explanation: If someone receives benefits unjustly, action should be taken against this.

207009.explanation: For livability and safety, it is good to design more streets in Groningen as “cars as guests” zones and to provide more space for cyclists and pedestrians.

207010.explanation: If residents ask for more space for play, social interaction, and green areas, parking spaces could be used for that purpose. Reducing parking spaces is not a goal in itself.

207011.explanation: Paid parking in urban neighborhoods with high parking pressure is acceptable in order to safeguard parking spaces for residents and visitors. It is important to continue evaluating this to determine whether the measure still aligns with its intended purpose.

207012.explanation: It is important to facilitate cycling in even more places by providing additional bicycle parking, which is why we should continue with the bicycle platforms.

207013.explanation: We must take care of creation and the environment; environmental zones contribute to this.

207014.explanation: Within neighborhoods and villages, we do support a 30 km/h speed limit. On major through roads, a 50 km/h limit is sufficient, provided the road is designed accordingly.

207015.explanation: We want exit programs to be generously funded and to ensure that people who wish to leave prostitution are given genuine prospects for the future.

207016.explanation: We consider it a positive initiative in terms of social interaction and sustainability. Therefore, we want to support residents who wish to realize this.

206949.explanation: D66 strongly supports involving residents in municipal policy. It's important that people have a voice in issues that affect them directly.

206950.explanation: The handling of the earthquake dossier is causing significant pain and sorrow among residents and needs to be organised better and more efficiently. It is important that residents have one clear point of clear, but it is not necessary that the municipality organises this.

206951.explanation: Groningen has been among the municipalities with the highest municipal taxes for several years, and the property tax (OZB) is a key contributor to that. D66 believes we should lower the property tax when the financial situation allows.

206952.explanation: Groningen is a student city, with students accounting for one in four residents. For that reason, it's important to actively involve students in policies that affect them. Students often encounter specific issues that are best addressed by other students.

206953.explanation: Sustainability is high on our agenda. D66 believes the municipality should be a driving force and good example in this area. Therefore, the municipality should purchase in a circular, sustainable manner, preferably locally.

206954.explanation: As far as D66 is concerned, cultural subsidies should be equally accessible to everyone. We make no distinction.

206955.explanation: Youth care is under pressure and D66 believes that reforms are essential. Blind cuts are the wrong approach. D66 wants to focus on efficiency, prevention, and working together to put youth care back in order.

206956.explanation: D66 believes that Groningen’s current policy strikes the right balance between shielding residents and shoppers from panhandling on the one hand, and actively helping those experiencing homelessness on the other. We do not believe any legal adjustments are necessary at this time.

206957.explanation: Both an ambitious climate policy and affordable healthcare are important for D66. For that reason, it's important not to pit them against each other.

206958.explanation: D66 strives for a green, sustainable municipality and wants to actively support businesses in this. Rejecting businesses outright because they still drive diesel vans or use central heating boilers, for example, is undesirable.

206959.explanation: D66 strongly supports more outdoor seating terraces in the municipality and wants to make it easier for businesses to create one. Businesses already face significant costs, such as the unnecessary advertising tax, so a more reasonable rate for terraces would be a welcome concession.

206960.explanation: Business owners should be able to advertise their own businesses. D66 believes it's wrong that they have to pay taxes for displaying their company name on their facade.

206961.explanation: Unequal investment leads to equal opportunities. For D66, it shouldn't matter where you were born or where you come from. To create opportunities, municipalities sometimes have to be willing to invest more in certain areas.

206962.explanation: Being able to get a croissant on a Sunday morning. For D66, it's a small symbol of the freedom that businesses should be able to decide their own business hours. Ultimately, proprietors are best capable to judge what’s right for them.

206963.explanation: The reception crisis in the asylum system affects us all. D66 wants every municipality to contribute to resolving it.

206964.explanation: People with urgent needs are given priority for social housing, for example in cases of divorce, medical problems, or impending homelessness. D66 believes this should also include status holders. This will also benefit the flow in reception centres.

206965.explanation: D66 believes that people without a residence permit should also be provided with shelter. Working towards repatriation is essential, but in Groningen, no one should be left to sleep in the cold.

206966.explanation: In practice, the link between work and housing for migrant workers often leads to exploitation and undesirable situations. D66 wants to eliminate this link.

206967.explanation: The Stadjerspas is intended for people with limited income. This does not necessarily apply to all refugees. When those who can afford it pay their own way, we keep our services accessible to everyone.

206968.explanation: D66 strives for a green, sustainable municipality and wants to actively support businesses in this. Rejecting businesses outright because they still drive diesel vans or use central heating boilers, for example, is undesirable. D66 supports an open and innovative investment climate.

206969.explanation: We can question the consequences of angling for animals and nature. D66 wants an open discussion about this, but a general ban is not in order.

206970.explanation: D66 supports proprietors' freedom to make their terraces more attractive with heating. However, old-fashioned systems such as those running on gas or other fossil fuels, are outdated. D66 wants to help these businesses make their terraces more sustainable.

206971.explanation: D66 strongly supports facilitating biodiversity. Mowing roadside verges and public grass less often, provided that it’s appropriate and traffic-safe, is essential to achieving this.

206972.explanation: Disconnecting homes from the gas grid is sometimes unavoidable when installing district heating, because a gas pipe can’t be left in place for just a few homes. For D66, a key condition is that these households won’t face higher energy bills.

206973.explanation: Groningen has the cheapest heating network in the country because the municipality ensures prices remain affordable. However, D66 does support giving other government bodies a share to limit the risks for the municipality.

206974.explanation: Ideally, solar panels should be placed on roofs and parking lots. However, for a good energy mix, it's sometimes necessary to install panels on fields, such as agricultural land. Placing them on residential properties is the preferred option.

206975.explanation: A green municipality is a healthy municipality. That's why D66 strives for plenty of new, extra greenery in the municipality of Groningen.

206976.explanation: Groningen needs to become vibrant again. D66 believes the current rules for events are too strict and too rigidly enforced. We believe events should be given more space to make our municipality even more appealing and vibrant.

206977.explanation: D66 wants to actively encourage events to become more sustainable, and setting requirements could be a part of this. However, only allowing 100% sustainable events would damage Groningen's vibrant character.

206978.explanation: There should be a place in the city for people who have experienced something distressing or need help. That's why D66 strongly supports safe spaces in nightlife areas. A safe night out includes getting home safe.

206979.explanation: D66 wants to make Groningen a vibrant city. We believe the current strict rules should be relaxed.

206980.explanation: It's a fact that people use drugs. That's why D66 wants people to have easy access to testing to ensure that their drugs are safe. By doing so, we can protect users from hazardous trash.

206981.explanation: A well-intentioned proposal, but one with significant financial consequences. D66 believes that those who can afford it should also pay for their own children. In doing so, we can ensure proper support for those who truly need it.

206982.explanation: Commemorating the history of slavery is more than just a moment's reflection each year. It's also about raising awareness of our role in an ugly part of Dutch colonial history. Naming streets and squares after resistance freedom fighters can play an important role in this.

206983.explanation: Commiserating our colonial past is crucial. Therefore, D66 supports having descendants of enslaved people organize an annual commiseration with the municipality's support. It's important that the municipality doesn't take over, but rather empowers residents to take the initiative.

206984.explanation: D66 does not participate in symbol politics. Both the carbide shooting and the Easter bonfires are annual events, not everyday occurrences, and they’re important to the residents involved. As far as we're concerned, the municipality shouldn't get in the way.

206986.explanation: Art and culture aren't just hobbies; they're essential for personal development, societal understanding and civic participation. D66 wants to introduce as many children as possible to the artistic and cultural offerings of our municipality.

206987.explanation: Our "Trots van het Noorden"; FC Groningen is a unifying force in Groningen and an important partner for the municipality. D66 supports the democratic 50+1 model, in which club members play a leading role. This could also mean that the municipality has to take a step back in control of the club.

206988.explanation: D66 supports a large pop music venue near Groningen's city center. However, we would prefer it to be part of a large, integrated music center that provides a platform for both pop and classical music plus musical education. Only then will we truly do justice to its cultural importance for Groningen.

206989.explanation: The police have a monopoly on the use of force and D66 wants to keep it that way. Keeping our enforcement officers and the public safe is a high priority for us, but increasing the use of force isn't a solution we support.

206990.explanation: D66 wants the municipality to be an employer that's welcoming to everyone and an important part of that is that people who work for the municipality can be themselves. D66 judges the neutrality of enforcement officers by their behavior, not their appearance.

206991.explanation: If more enforcement capacity is needed, D66 is absolutely open to discussing it. Simply doubling the capacity is an untargeted measure with significant financial consequences.

206992.explanation: D66 has concerns about both the effectiveness and privacy implications of increased camera surveillance. At the same time, it's always up for discussion, but other options (such as improved lighting or redesigning public spaces) should be properly explored first.

206993.explanation: Everyone should have the same opportunities to find housing. For D66, it doesn't matter whether you already have a connection to Groningen or want to develop one. Moreover, a measure such as this is bad for the municipality’s business climate.

206994.explanation: D66 is open to discussing priority for essential professions. However, it's not currently necessary in Groningen and the focus should be on having sufficient housing. Furthermore, such a measure would only be able to help a very limited group, given the income threshold for social housing.

206995.explanation: D66 wants to address the housing crisis swiftly and effectively. No solution is perfect. D66 does, however, believe it's important that buildings blend in well with their surroundings.

206996.explanation: D66 sees this requirement as a small part of the solution to the housing crisis, but only in neighbourhoods and city districts where this is necessary. For us, it should be an option only when truly necessary, such as in the city centre and its surrounding neighbourhoods with many students.

206997.explanation: Empty properties are detrimental to the appearance and vibrant character of our municipality. Therefore, D66 believes it's important to combat vacancy actively, provided that it's feasible for the owner.

206998.explanation: There are cases where the building regulations have led to undesirable outcomes, such as the refusal to provide a permit for a mural on Eikenlaan. At the same time, the building regulations are crucial for safeguarding authenticity and character, for example, in our villages.

206999.explanation: D66 wants to invest more in affordable student housing. It's important to build more student houses and fewer expensive, anonymous studios. D66 supports rent subsidies, including for student rooms.

207000.explanation: D66 wants to address the housing crisis quickly and effectively. No single solution is perfect. A large part of the housing crisis can be solved with a small portion of agricultural land. Farmers should always receive proper and fair compensation.

207002.explanation: D66 supports the 30-40-30 principle. Every neighborhood should have at least 30% social housing, with a maximum of 50%. This creates a unified city.

207003.explanation: D66 wants to encourage people on welfare to do volunteer work, but making it mandatory is not an option. There are far too many complicated and unclear regulations surrounding welfare, which D66 wants to eliminate.

207004.explanation: D66 doesn't see sheltered employment as a final point, but rather as a springboard to regular employment. While sheltered employment can be a solution for a very limited group, D66 isn't considering expanding this.

207006.explanation: D66 prioritizes personal autonomy when it comes to choosing a job, and the job offered must be a good fit for the person. D66 wants to encourage people to work, not punish them for being on welfare.

207007.explanation: Policies like these are important in creating opportunities. D66 advocates providing support to families with low incomes, so that all children have the same opportunities in education and in life.

207008.explanation: It's important to handle public funds fairly. At the same time, D66 believes it's important to remember that receiving help from others, such as occasional groceries, doesn't constitute fraud. D66 sees no reason to change current policy.

207009.explanation: Car-free streets improve the quality of life and make cycling more attractive. D66 wants to implement more of these types of streets in the municipality.

207010.explanation: D66 wants to assess whether the current number of parking spots is still necessary in residential areas and near the city center. Less parking spots means more greenery, room for children to play, and improved quality of life. Parking garages and shopping centers should remain easily accessible.

207011.explanation: Parking in the municipality of Groningen is expensive enough and as far as D66 is concerned, these rates don't need to be raised any further. We want to invest the proceeds in good bicycle parking and improving public transport.

207012.explanation: For D66, the bicycle is number one. It's therefore important that there's always enough space to park your bike safely in the city center.

207013.explanation: D66 believes in a reliable government. This also means adhering to decisions when businesses need to invest. D66 wants sound transition arrangements and ongoing dialogue with proprietors. National incentives for zero-emission transport are essential.

207014.explanation: 30 km/h roads are beneficial for road safety and quality of life and should be widely implemented. However, traffic flow is also an important consideration on many main roads, and 50 km/h is a more logical speed limit in those instances.

207015.explanation: D66 considers sex work to be a legitimate profession and sex workers' self-determination is important. With that in mind, it's important that effective programs are in place to help people quit should they wish to.

207016.explanation: D66 believes food gardens are a great initiative that should primarily be led by residents. Initiatives with bottom-up community support are preferred, and the municipality should only play a facilitating role.

206949.explanation: The VVD is against a citizens' assembly. In our opinion it is too costly and ineffective. The city council is elected to represent residents and has the time and resources to prepare, read and include stakeholders to make a balanced decision. Therefore the responsibility should lie with the council.

206950.explanation: The VVD is in favour of a single municipal team where people can arrange everything regarding earthquake damage. The current system is too convoluted and complex for citizens. The handling of damage cases must be as clear and easy as possible.

206951.explanation: The VVD is in favour of lowering the property tax. The municipality is currently spending too much money. By budgeting smarter and more efficiently, the property tax can go down. Lower local burdens give Groningers more peace of mind in their finances.

206952.explanation: The VVD is against a separate student affairs department, because students are full-fledged residents and do not need a separate department. Extra departments mean more civil servants and costs, but not automatically better service. That is why we prefer to invest in safety, housing construction, an

206953.explanation: The VVD is against the idea that the municipality must primarily purchase circularly. Green policy must be realistic and must not lead to unnecessarily higher costs or the exclusion of Groningen companies. We choose green where possible, but with an eye for innovation and fair competition.

206954.explanation: The VVD believes that subsidies should not go exclusively to heritage and local traditions. There are more cultural institutions that have great social and economic value. A broad cultural offering strengthens the economy, attracts visitors, and keeps Groningen attractive for young and old.

206955.explanation: The VVD is against a ban on budget cuts and prefers to see smart savings. Not every problem requires professional youth care and can be solved at home. By preventing everything from being medicalized, we keep youth care available for families who really need it.

206956.explanation: The VVD is in favour of the begging ban. Groningen is confronted with organized begging gangs. We should not facilitate that, but actively fight it. With the ban, we protect vulnerable people and strengthen the liveability and safety in the municipality.

206957.explanation: The VVD opposes the simple shifting of budgets. The municipality must make smart choices within both domains, so that climate measures have the desired effect and care reaches those who really need it. Good governance is more than shifting money around.

206958.explanation: The VVD is against this unrealistic proposal. Virtually all companies still use fossil energy, including the Groningen SME (MKB) sector. Moreover, this ban is unrealistically difficult to monitor in practice. The municipality must focus on its core tasks instead of symbolic politics.

206959.explanation: The VVD wants to stop squeezing hospitality businesses dry with the sky-high terrace tax. The hardworking entrepreneurs provide a lot of employment and a lively municipality; they should not be fined but rather embraced.

206960.explanation: The advertising tax is nothing more than bullying entrepreneurs. Moreover, it is incredibly labour-intensive and expensive to enforce. The VVD wants to abolish this "display window tax" as soon as possible.

206961.explanation: The VVD is against investing in one neighbourhood at the expense of another. Liveability in all neighbourhoods and villages are equally important. We achieve that by investing everywhere in maintenance, safety, and a clean public space.

206962.explanation: The VVD wants entrepreneurs to be free in determining opening and closing times. Every entrepreneur should be free to choose whether they want to be open on Sunday morning or not. Additionally, the VVD is in favour of maintaining unrestricted opening times for hospitality entrepreneurs.

206963.explanation: The VVD believes that the Netherlands must offer shelter to real refugees, but that this must not be at the expense of the liveability and safety of Groningen. That means that there must be a good balance in the number of temporary shelter locations, which is currently at its maximum.

206964.explanation: The VVD is against this priority. People with strong ties to Groningen who have lived and worked here for years should not be sent to the back of the line. Social rental homes are for those who need them most, not based on origin.

206965.explanation: The national government has chosen to stop sheltering persons with a permit and no longer provides financial resources. Currently the municipality pays for this undesirable shelter itself; we must stop that. Municipal money is not intended for the shelter of people without a residence permit.

206966.explanation: A housing obligation for employers makes migrant workers too dependent. However, we should invest in enough housing of migrant workers. The municipality must strictly enforce against rogue companies that exploit migrant workers.

206967.explanation: The VVD is against automatically granting the Stadjerspas to refugees. Everyone must be treated equally. The pass is intended for residents who meet the same income and condition requirements, regardless of background or origin.

206968.explanation: The VVD is against this proposal. Groningen needs jobs and economic activity. We encourage sustainability, but we do not exclude companies that cannot yet fully take that step. Unfeasible demands cost jobs and drive companies away.

206969.explanation: The VVD is in favour of angling. Anglers with a valid fishing pass enjoy their hobby in nature and cause no nuisance. The VVD wants people to choose in freedom how they spend their free time.

206970.explanation: The VVD is against banning terrace heating. Hospitality entrepreneurs must be able to determine for themselves how they make their terrace attractive. The municipality must facilitate entrepreneurs, not thwart them.

206971.explanation: A clean, green, and well-maintained living environment is not a luxury, but a basic condition. Grass that is too long causes dilapidation and unsafe traffic situations. We choose a well-groomed municipality, with neat verges and without crooked paving stones.

206972.explanation: The VVD is against mandatory gas disconnection. Going off-gas must be feasible and affordable, not imposed by the municipality. We support voluntary sustainability, but we do not accept coercion and unaffordable bills for Groningers.

206973.explanation: The VVD wants to stop playing entrepreneur. Warmtestad entails very large financial risks. We are looking at which parts can be divested so that the municipality can focus on core tasks instead of acting like an energy company.

206974.explanation: As far as the VVD is concerned, solar panels belong on roofs and not on agricultural land. We utilize roofs of homes and businesses and cover parking lots with solar panels, so that precious nature and agricultural land are preserved.

206975.explanation: The VVD supports the planting of new forest. More greenery improves the living environment, offers space for recreation, and strengthens biodiversity. We look for suitable locations without hindering housing construction or business activity.

206976.explanation: The VVD wants more space for events. A vibrant city requires flexibility. Organizers must be able to let events continue longer where possible, with an eye for the surroundings and liveability.

206977.explanation: The VVD is against excluding events that are not 100% sustainable. These kinds of demands make events unaffordable and drive organizers away. We encourage sustainability, but no absolute bans. A vibrant city asks for room for initiatives, not for restrictive rules.

206978.explanation: The VVD wants a safe nightlife and focuses on measures that make the entire nightlife area safer: more camera surveillance, designating the city centre as a security risk area, and more police on the street. Everyone must feel safe while going out; a safe space can help.

206979.explanation: The VVD does not want fewer, but more festivals. Events make Groningen vibrant and attractive for residents (Stadjers), students, and visitors. They provide employment and economic activity.

206980.explanation: The VVD is against normalizing drug use and checking prohibited substances at the expense of the community. The VVD wants to confront users with the world behind drugs, for example with campaigns like: "Your drug use blows up porches."

206981.explanation: The VVD is against paying sports memberships for all children. Help must be aimed at families who really need it, so that facilities remain affordable and effective.

206982.explanation: The VVD is against mandatorily prescribing one theme for street names. Resistance heroes from the slavery past deserve recognition, but this must not become a fixed starting point.

206983.explanation: The VVD believes that commemorations must come from social organizations, such as the Stichting 4 Mei Herdenking Groningen, not from the municipality. We facilitate initiatives but do not take charge ourselves.

206984.explanation: The VVD is in favour of preserving local traditions such as carbide shooting and Easter fires. Groningen is a municipality with a large city, but we must not forget our villages. Events such as the Easter fires and carbide shooting bring people in our villages together. We must cherish these traditi

206986.explanation: We believe that children should get acquainted with art and culture, but the municipality currently spends too much money on art and culture. The VVD believes that the money must be used smarter so that as many children as possible can get acquainted with it. More money is not the solution.

206987.explanation: The VVD wants to give FC Groningen as much freedom as possible. Whether supporters become co-owners or a commercial investor joins is up to the club and not the municipality. The municipality must work on a good relationship with the club.

206988.explanation: Groningen deserves a vibrant cultural centre. We choose the most profitable option for the new music centre. The two-location solution, where a pop venue is built behind the station AND the current Oosterpoort is renovated, is in principle a good idea.

206989.explanation: The VVD is in favour of equipping BOAs with a baton and pepper spray. Enforcers must be able to defend themselves against aggression. This increases their safety and makes enforcement more effective. BOAs do important work on the street and deserve the right tools to do so safely.

206990.explanation: The VVD is against religious expressions for BOAs. Enforcers represent the neutral government and must be able to act without bias. Religious symbols in the uniform affect that neutrality. In this way, we guarantee the unity and impartiality of municipal enforcement.

206991.explanation: The VVD wants more visible enforcers in neighbourhoods and villages. Doubling is not a goal in itself, but extra capacity ensures a faster approach to nuisance and a safer feeling on the street.

206992.explanation: The VVD has been fighting for years for more and better cameras in the city centre. Camera surveillance increases the chance of catching perpetrators and helps to tackle nuisance. The current cameras are often of poor quality. Safety comes first, so we invest in modern, extensive camera surveillance

206993.explanation: The owner of a home chooses to whom a home is rented, not the municipality. The VVD believes that a distinction must be made between private and social rental homes. The VVD believes that people with local ties should receive priority in the allocation of a social rental home.

206994.explanation: The VVD is against priority rules based on profession. Social rental homes are for people who really need them, not for specific occupational groups. We choose local ties and urgency as criteria, not the work someone does. Whoever needs it most comes first.

206995.explanation: The VVD is not afraid to go up in height. On a small surface area, we can add many homes by building higher. Think of high residential towers behind the Main Station. This way we solve the housing shortage without building over precious greenery. "Build, build, build" also means building upwards.

206996.explanation: The VVD is against further government interference in the housing market. The buy-out protection has actually caused the supply of private rental homes to disappear. This hits starters who cannot get a mortgage and have no right to social housing. Market disruption solves nothing; building more does

206997.explanation: The VVD is against a vacancy tax. Properties often stand empty because owners are waiting for slow municipal permits for renovation. Instead of taxing owners extra, the municipality must issue permits much faster and work together instead of working against them.

206998.explanation: The VVD chooses more freedom and faster building and less power for the aesthetics committee. We are making a new aesthetics policy with minimal rules, so that residents decide for themselves what their house looks like while simultaneously protecting heritage.

206999.explanation: The VVD wants more student homes, but not with municipal money. We give room to project developers and investors to build. Through fewer rules, faster permits, and broader conversion permits, more student rooms will appear automatically. The market solves this, not the municipal treasury.

207000.explanation: The VVD chooses to build in existing neighbourhoods AND expansions at the edges of the municipality. This way we solve the housing shortage, while valuable green areas remain preserved.

207002.explanation: The VVD wants a better spread of social rental homes. Some neighbourhoods now consist of more than 50% social rent; that is out of balance. We make agreements with housing corporations to make neighbourhoods more mixed and hold talks with neighbouring municipalities about regional distribution.

207003.explanation: The VVD believes that whoever can work, must work. Socially useful work in exchange for benefits is reasonable and helps people take a step towards the labour market. We have an eye for those who truly cannot, but whoever can must contribute to society.

207004.explanation: We do not believe in subsidized jobs that compete with our entrepreneurs. The VVD chooses real jobs. Work must pay off and offer perspective, not be a dead end job at the expense of the taxpayer.

207006.explanation: Benefits are a safety net, not a hammock. Whoever can work but refuses, gets cut. We do not accept that people lean back at the expense of society while entrepreneurs cannot find staff. The VVD chooses a hard approach: whoever does not want to participate will feel it in their wallet.

207007.explanation: The VVD wants to get rid of separate schemes that increase dependence. We do not solve poverty by handing out stuff, but by making work pay, so that parents can pay for facilities for their children themselves. There is sufficient room within the special assistance (bijzondere bijstand).

207008.explanation: Benefit fraud is theft from the hardworking Groninger. We do not tolerate abuse of our social system. The reporting point must remain, and we are betting heavily on enforcement. Every euro that goes to a fraudster cannot go to someone who really needs it. We tackle fraudsters very hard.

207009.explanation: The car is not an unwanted guest, but a means of transport that many Groningers depend on. The VVD stops bullying motorists away. We separate traffic flows for safety, instead of throwing everything onto one pile. Motorists deserve space, not obstacles.

207010.explanation: The VVD actually wants MORE parking spots so that everyone can find a place for their car in the evening after work. For example, through a 'Parking Spots Plan'. We do not chase visitors and residents out of the municipality. The Kattendiep parking garage must simply reopen after new construction.

207011.explanation: The car is not a cash cow for left-wing hobby policies. Parking rates in Groningen are sky-high and must go down. Parking must remain affordable for residents, visitors, and entrepreneurs. We stop squeezing motorists dry and ensure that the city remains accessible and hospitable.

207012.explanation: The VVD does not choose the bicycle or the car, but both. We want more bicycle parking spots, but not by removing parking spaces for cars. The city centre must be accessible to everyone. We create extra space instead of thwarting motorists and entrepreneurs.

207013.explanation: The VVD is fiercely against new zero-emission zones that drive entrepreneurs into costs and exclude people with an older car. We choose feasible sustainability without coercion. Everyone must remain welcome in our city, even without an electric car.

207014.explanation: Important arterial roads will remain 50 km/h. We are not going to bully motorists with unnecessarily slow driving. Where we introduce 30 km/h, we don't just hang up a sign, but we actually design the road that way. No false sense of safety, but flow where possible and safety where necessary.

207015.explanation: The VVD believes that support for exiting is important, but sees no need for extra municipal budget. Existing help must be deployed effectively, with a focus on safety, guidance, and combating abuses.

207016.explanation: The VVD applauds initiatives by residents. Food gardens stimulate social cohesion in neighbourhoods. However, the municipality does not have to finance this. Tax money is intended for core tasks such as safety and a clean living environment.

206949.explanation: It is vital that political parties engage directly with residents as much as possible. In some cases, a citizens' assembly can strengthen the city council's decision-making process. However, we do not want to impose this as a mandatory requirement.

206950.explanation: Residents facing earthquake damage must receive swift, high-quality support for foundation issues, repairs, and reinforcement. Currently, aid is fragmented. We believe a single municipal team could help ensure residents receive faster and better assistance.

206951.explanation: It is impossible to lower property taxes in the coming years; we cannot fulfill that promise. However, we must stop increasing them. By only allowing inflation adjustments from now on, we ensure the tax burden on homeowners remains manageable.

206952.explanation: We want to create a government service point where students, guided by experienced officials, support locals. This includes help with income support, language lessons, and applying for healthcare or student benefits.

206953.explanation: The municipality of Groningen already prioritizes circular and sustainable procurement. We want to maintain this approach while ensuring that products and services remain affordable.

206954.explanation: Our Groningen heritage and traditions tell a unique story that we must protect and strengthen—from carbidschieten to Bommen Berend. Other cultural activities also deserve attention. Protecting this means avoiding the heavy budget cuts we want to prevent.

206955.explanation: The municipality receives insufficient funds to fully finance youth care. We have the responsibility but lack the budget. We cannot afford cuts or longer waiting lists. It is essential to continue investing heavily in these services.

206956.explanation: We support the homeless in Groningen through new day and night shelters and benefits. This ban enables police to intervene when begging causes a nuisance.

206957.explanation: This is not possible. The municipality receives specific grants from the State and the EU for climate goals and the energy transition. These funds are "allocated," meaning they are legally restricted and cannot be spent on anything else.

206958.explanation: Sustainability and caring for our world are vital to the CDA. Therefore, we will continue to encourage Groningen businesses and partners to go green. This "Green Pressure" is more effective than imposing these types of bans.

206959.explanation: The CDA believes in supporting local businesses. However, this tax is legally fixed. Under EU rules, the tax must equal the costs of licensing and enforcement. Currently, there is a minor discrepancy, so it must be slightly reduced.

206960.explanation: This advertising tax adds to the already high costs for our entrepreneurs. We believe a cleaner streetscape shouldn't require extra fees that hit small local businesses hard. Instead, we should work with entrepreneurs rather than against them.

206961.explanation: We want to maintain and improve livability in all our neighborhoods and villages. This requires investing where it’s needed most. Sometimes, that means allocating more funds to poorer areas simply because the need there is greater.

206962.explanation: We advocate for more freedom regarding business opening hours. Every shopkeeper and entrepreneur should be able to make their own choice. As a municipality, we should not seek to enforce these restrictions.

206963.explanation: As long as there is no structural solution for shelter, we believe we must help and house those in need. This is why emergency shelters exist. Every municipality must take its responsibility in this matter.

206964.explanation: By changing our priority rules, we ensure better flow and a longer housing chain. This benefits all residents, including this group. The CDA is committed to prioritizing residents with local ties. This way, we can free up more living space faster.

206965.explanation: People whose asylum applications have been denied must return to their country of origin. In the meantime, the municipality should not stop providing basic shelter. It is unwise to let these people disappear into illegality without any support.

206966.explanation: Companies requiring migrant workers must provide proper housing. There are currently distressing examples where this fails. Therefore, the municipality must strictly monitor and prevent these abuses.

206967.explanation: Low-income residents receive a Stadjerspas. Formally, refugees are not residents until they obtain legal status. Once they become residents of our municipality, they can use the pass if needed.

206968.explanation: Sustainability is vital, and we aim to encourage it among businesses and partners. This is more effective than imposing mandates. Other factors, like employment, are also very important. Therefore, we do not want to exclude companies beforehand.

206969.explanation: Fishing and magnet fishing should remain possible in designated areas.

206970.explanation: We must cherish our vibrant city center and our hospitality entrepreneurs. Therefore, we oppose a ban, preferring to let business owners make their own choices on this matter.

206971.explanation: We believe a tidy public space is important. In many locations, maintenance needs to be improved.

206972.explanation: We must phase out gas step-by-step and transition to sustainable heat sources. Gas emissions cannot continue indefinitely, especially as it will eventually become unaffordable. We are assessing sustainability per neighborhood to ensure energy bills for Groningen residents decrease.

206973.explanation: We are open to a strategic partner buying a minority stake in Warmtestad. A full sale is unwise as it leads to higher energy bills for residents and eventually opens the door to foreign owners.

206974.explanation: We are easing permit requirements for farmers. This makes it easier for agricultural entrepreneurs to become multifunctional. From power generation and farm shops to residential zoning, we are returning entrepreneurial freedom to our farmers.

206975.explanation: We want more parks and forests in our municipality. This improves air quality and adds many recreational opportunities.

206976.explanation: Groningen is a vibrant city with many events in and around the center. We want to preserve that. However, our city is compact, and near the Suikerterrein and Stadspark, many residents eventually need their sleep. Therefore, we will maintain the current operating hours.

206977.explanation: Sustainability is important to us, and we are committed to encouraging it. This is more effective than imposing mandates. We want to protect and strengthen local festivities in our neighborhoods and villages, while encouraging them to be as sustainable as possible.

206978.explanation: We believe it is important that our residents and guests feel safe and are safe in our municipality. Therefore, we are creating a safe space in the nightlife district. Additionally, we are expanding CCTV surveillance in the city center and granting the police the authority to conduct preventive sear

206979.explanation: We are proud of our events. Eurosonic Noorderslag, Noorderzon, Summerstage, and Hullabaloo are examples of festivals that bring people together and connect us. We are maintaining current end times to limit nuisance for local residents.

206980.explanation: This shows that, as a municipality, we are normalizing the use of hard drugs. Usage has grown immensely and is visibly making the nightlife more grim. Our primary focus is on securing strong enforcement powers to tackle drug trafficking, possession, and use in the city center.

206981.explanation: Sports foster connection, community, and health. This should be accessible to everyone, not just those who can afford it. Exercising at a young age helps prevent obesity in childhood and adulthood. Additionally, it is an investment in a healthy society with lower healthcare costs.

206982.explanation: We believe it is important to honor resistance heroes from the history of slavery. However, there are many heroes, and we believe they all deserve recognition. Therefore, we want to make it possible for other heroes as well to have new streets and squares named after them.

206983.explanation: The municipality now supports the annual Keti Koti celebration in Groningen, and as far as we are concerned, we will continue to do so.

206984.explanation: Our traditions are under pressure. We consider them indispensable. They bring us together and tell the story of Groningen. Therefore, we want to protect and strengthen the Easter fires and carbidschieten.

206986.explanation: By investing in arts and culture for children, we ensure access for everyone. it helps children discover their talents and contributes to problem-solving skills. We also introduce children to our local Groningen culture and traditions.

206987.explanation: Our pride of the north should not fall into the hands of foreign investors. That is why we are open to a model in which supporters and regional entrepreneurs invest and acquire shares of ownership in FC Groningen.

206988.explanation: We want to build a new large concert hall. However, we believe it is important that this does not become an excessive expense, ensuring funds remain available for other investments. The location near the station is our preference, provided it stays within the financial frameworks.

206989.explanation: Our BOAs (law enforcement officers) are indispensable to our safety. We must better protect them to further strengthen their authority and effectiveness on the streets. Therefore, we are providing our BOAs with a baton and a bodycam.

206990.explanation: Prohibiting the wearing of religious symbols by the police encourages discrimination. A ban suggests that officials who visibly express their religion cannot be neutral. We believe in the professionalism of our law enforcement officers and must respect that freedom.

206991.explanation: There is a shortage of police officers, and this problem cannot be solved overnight. To ensure sufficient law enforcement in the meantime, an interim solution could be to deploy more BOAs (municipal law enforcement officers).

206992.explanation: CCTV surveillance can contribute to the safety of our residents and enforcement. Because crime is increasing in our municipality, we want to expand camera surveillance. This allows us to support the police, making it easier for them to intervene and track down offenders.

206993.explanation: We previously proposed giving priority to residents with local ties. This creates flow and a longer relocation chain. In this way, a single vacant property leads to more moves toward suitable housing.

206994.explanation: We want to make it easier for people in crucial professions in our municipality to find a suitable home here.

206995.explanation: There is a major housing shortage in our municipality, but of course, space is limited. High-rise flats can help create more homes. Naturally, this must remain within reason and always take the surrounding area into account, so it does not detract too much from our beautiful municipality.

206996.explanation: The interests of home seekers are our priority. That is why we are expanding the self-occupancy obligation. By doing so, we are pushing investors and slumlords out of our municipality. However, we believe it is important that it remains possible to purchase a home for immediate family members.

206997.explanation: There is a major housing shortage in our municipality. It is unjustifiable that buildings remain empty while many people are searching for a home. Therefore, we are introducing a vacancy ordinance, ensuring it is no longer profitable to keep a property vacant instead of selling it.

206998.explanation: We consider it very important that sufficient housing remains available for our residents. However, the aesthetic building requirements (welstandsregels) ensure that our municipality also remains a beautiful place to live.

206999.explanation: We want to provide a structural solution to the student housing shortage. Therefore, we have chosen to increase the intended number of student housing units on the Zernike Campus from 1,500 to 2,500.

207000.explanation: We are easing permit requirements for farmers. This makes it easier for agricultural entrepreneurs to become multifunctional. From power generation and farm shops to residential zoning, we are returning entrepreneurial freedom to our farmers.

207002.explanation: Our municipality thrives on its diversity of incomes and backgrounds. To preserve this, we advocate for inclusive neighborhoods with a wide variety of housing types, ensuring there is a place for everyone in every district.

207003.explanation: We advocate for a society where all residents are involved. For those who are able, work provides structure, social interaction, and social engagement. This can limit long-term welfare dependency and reduce the gap to finding a new job.

207004.explanation: It is vital that residents unable to hold regular jobs can still contribute to society. However, creating more basic jobs (basisbanen) beyond our current goal is not feasible, as we simply cannot find enough suitable candidates at this time.

207006.explanation: It is in the interest of both the municipality and welfare recipients to work. Under existing rules, those who refuse a suitable job offer while on benefits can face a reduction in their welfare payments.

207007.explanation: We advocate for equal opportunities for every child. By supporting costs for school, bicycles, and laptops, we contribute to that equality. Our municipality already provides this support, and we must continue to do so.

207008.explanation: We believe it is important that residents have the opportunity to report fraud whenever they encounter it. Together, this allows us to contribute to a fair and honest society.

207009.explanation: Many residents of Groningen rely on their cars for work and family care. For some, especially those with mobility issues, using a car and having nearby parking is essential.

207010.explanation: Many residents rely on their cars for work and family. For those with mobility issues, nearby parking is essential. We also believe it is important that residents remain able to receive visitors.

207011.explanation: Parking rates have risen sharply. For residents with mobility issues, nearby parking is essential. We also believe that receiving visitors must remain affordable.

207012.explanation: It shouldn't be a choice between the two. We need space to park bicycles neatly and nearby, but this shouldn't come at the expense of the already limited number of car parking spaces.

207013.explanation: We will continue to encourage sustainability step by step. That is why we are generous with exemptions for entrepreneurs.

207014.explanation: We value road safety for residents of all ages. It is proven that lowering the speed limit to 30 km/h reduces traffic accidents and significantly enhances overall safety.

207015.explanation: Forced prostitution and human trafficking are serious crimes. The municipality must do its utmost to rescue victims. Programs for sex workers who wish to quit contribute to this effort.

207016.explanation: Community gardens foster meeting, cooperation, and connection throughout our municipality. We would like to expand these initiatives, but this does not have to happen everywhere.

206949.explanation: Volt wants to make use of advisory citizens’ assemblies more often, including at the neighbourhood level. The outcomes of a citizens’ assembly should be known before the city council makes a decision.

206950.explanation: Volt considers it important that damage compensation is arranged as conveniently as possible for residents. Damage compensation is already being handled by the IMG, and it is not clear to us whether adding a new municipal team would improve the process or instead delay it further.

206951.explanation: Volt does not want to lower the property tax but use it for everyone. We aim to use this tax revenue to implement fair social and economic policies.

206952.explanation: Volt wants educational institutions, students and the municipality to work together to support students, providing enough housing, clear information and opportunities to participate fully in student life.

206953.explanation: Volt promotes a circular economy to be less dependent on fossil resources. By buying as much as possible locally, we support local businesses and make Groningen economically strong and socially fair.

206954.explanation: Volt values heritage and local traditions, but also wants to support new talent with subsidies for artists and creators in performing arts, visual arts, literature, and film.

206955.explanation: Volt sees healthcare as a shared responsibility, not a cost. Young people deserve stability and good access to healthcare, without long waiting times or unnecessary referrals.

206956.explanation: Volt believes aggressive begging should remain banned, but a total ban goes too far. Our focus is on fighting poverty, providing shelter and offering help, not on punishment.

206957.explanation: The climate affects Groningen’s liveability. We must keep investing in climate plans, for example to prevent heat stress. Volt wants a sustainable Groningen with good healthcare and strong climate plans to keep the city liveable now and in the future.

206958.explanation: Volt wants to use fossil fuels only where necessary, with CO2-compensation through forests and sustainable agriculture. Advertising that promotes unhealthy behaviour or fossil fuels should be removed. Our focus is on local products and circular production.

206959.explanation: Volt wants terrace taxes to be fair and workable. Lowering taxes isn’t the goal; it’s about supporting businesses, keeping public space accessible, and using revenue for neighbourhoods and the city. This keeps the city lively and businesses viable.

206960.explanation: Volt thinks the advertising tax in Groningen is too complex and bureaucratic. We want to abolish it as it currently exists to make way for fairer and more workable policies.

206961.explanation: Volt wants to invest more in equality between neighbourhoods. City or village: Everyone deserves a good place to live. We aim for liveable, green communities across Groningen while preserving each area’s unique character.

206962.explanation: Volt wants to give shop owners flexible opening hours while keeping the city’s liveability in mind. We aim for vibrant streets that encourage interaction and entrepreneurship.

206963.explanation: Volt wants to scale down emergency shelters and invest in permanent, small-scale accommodation. This is cheaper and provides better living conditions, helping newcomers integrate more effectively.

206964.explanation: The municipality needs to be able to do this to meet its obligations. Faster processing is necessary to relieve the asylum system. This affects only about 6–10% of social housing and is not the cause of the housing shortage.

206965.explanation: Discontinuing shelter services is not the solution, as it would leave people on the streets and make oversight harder. Everyone deserves shelter, food, and access to healthcare, regardless of residence status.

206966.explanation: Volt believes employers should work with the municipality to provide good housing for migrant workers, while migrants should still have the freedom to choose where they live.

206967.explanation: Volt believes newcomers should receive a Stadjerspas, as it helps them integrate. The Stadjerspas would allow them to participate more quickly in the community, for example in sports or other activities.

206968.explanation: Volt believes that prosperity is about more than profit. It’s about fairness, sustainability, and a lively city. That’s why Volt supports socially conscious and sustainable businesses, like bike-friendly employers.

206969.explanation: Volt does not want to ban recreational fishing entirely, but wants animal welfare and social goals to be protected. Angling associations also contribute to nature management and supervision along waterways, making a positive contribution.

206970.explanation: Volt wants to allow more sustainable terrace heating, like electric blankets, and encourages businesses to switch to green alternatives. The social role of terraces continues beyond the summer.

206971.explanation: Volt does not want to start mowing verges more often, to protect biodiversity. However, traffic safety must be maintained, invasive species controlled, and native plants preserved.

206972.explanation: Volt wants Groningen to be climate neutral by 2035. With smart devices, financial incentives, and campaigns, Volt encourages sustainable behaviour. Neighbourhoods that can go gas-free receive extra support and clear information through the insulation plan and Nij Begun.

206973.explanation: WarmteStad shows that Groningen can collectively own its own heat supply. This provides sustainable, gas-free heating, where profits in one neighbourhood help make other areas affordable to transition as well.

206974.explanation: Volt wants to help farmers switch to sustainable crops and local food chains. Solar panels, small wind turbines, and manure digesters support this. Cooperative solar parks and multifunctional land use also help make Groningen climate-neutral.

206975.explanation: Volt considers forest planting (like the Meerbos) important to connect nature areas and improve liveability, as described in Natuurnetwerk Nederland. This helps plants and animals survive and keeps nature viable.

206976.explanation: Volt wants to support culture and creativity. Early closing of events, like Paradigm, causes more nuisance as people continue their night elsewhere. Volt advocates better neighborhood consultation and more noise-control measures.

206977.explanation: For Volt, a good event is about connection, fairness, and sustainability. That’s why Volt supports sustainable, socially responsible entrepreneurs and cultural organizations that offer fair pay and help talent thrive in Groningen.

206978.explanation: Volt is in favour of permanent safe spaces. In this way, we offer a safe place and support every night for everyone who needs it.

206979.explanation: Volt does not want to reduce the number of festivals. We aim to grow culture with subsidies, good facilities, and input from creators, preserving icons, fostering creativity, and letting talent thrive in Groningen.

206980.explanation: Volt is in favour of a modern drug policy based on facts, health, and safety, and not fear or prejudice. Volt wants good education, as was already done in Utrecht with an educational “XTC shop.”

206981.explanation: Volt values (youth) sports for health and social cohesion. Children can already participate affordably through the Jeugdsportfonds or Stadjerspas, but additional contributions can be provided where needed so everyone can join.

206982.explanation: Volt believes it’s important that history is represented inclusively. The legacy of slavery is part of the region’s story and should be visible and acknowledged.

206983.explanation: Volt believes it’s important that history is represented inclusively. The legacy of slavery is part of the region’s story and should be visible and acknowledged.

206984.explanation: Volt values and supports local traditions and practices, and supports Easter fires and carbide shooting as long as safety, materials, and location are carefully coordinated with the municipality.

206986.explanation: Volt wants to invest in a municipality where everyone can develop from a young age. We will make certain museum days, films, and other experiences free to access. Volt sees culture as an investment that pays off for everyone.

206987.explanation: Volt wants supporters to become co-owners, just like the 50+1 model in Germany. This gives FC Groningen supporters influence and more responsibility for their club, and also prevents unwanted foreign investments.

206988.explanation: The organizations and artists that form the heart of our city must have certainty about their future. Volt supports the new pop venue, but also wants to explore whether other locations might be more suitable. This is necessary because the Lelylijn may later run along the south side of the station.

206989.explanation: Volt focuses on prevention and a strong social foundation. Together with residents and organizations, we aim to solve problems before they escalate, ensuring everyone feels safe without violence.

206990.explanation: Volt believes that public officials should reflect the diversity of society. Religious symbols should be included in uniform regulations to promote inclusivity and strengthen connections with all communities.

206991.explanation: Investing in more enforcement officers is necessary, but they aren’t always the right solution. Volt wants to prevent problems through prevention, a strong social foundation, and well-designed public spaces. Sufficient staff and appropriate enforcement ensure safety, including at nightlife venues.

206992.explanation: Volt wants camera surveillance only if it genuinely improves safety, because cameras often just shift crime without preventing it. Surveillance must be necessary, transparent, and privacy must be well protected.

206993.explanation: Volt wants to build 20,000 additional homes over the next ten years. These will be sustainable, affordable and suitable for everyone, and for all stages of life. We ensure a good mix of rental and owner-occupied homes in all price ranges, so people can move on within the housing market.

206994.explanation: Volt wants healthcare workers, teachers, and police to easily rent or buy affordable housing in Groningen, even though supply is scarce and expensive. This keeps the city accessible for these essential professionals.

206995.explanation: Volt believes flats over six stories are allowed if they fit the surroundings. With greenery, amenities, and resident input, Volt aims to preserve each neighbourhood’s identity and create pleasant living spaces.

206996.explanation: Volt believes homes should primarily be owner-occupied to prevent extended vacancy periods and maintain stability in the housing market. This helps more people find a home quickly and keeps housing fair and accessible for everyone.

206997.explanation: Volt supports an active policy that makes full use of the Vacancy Act. In addition to introducing a tax, Volt promotes new housing forms like friends’ housing, senior courtyards, and cooperatives, and experiments with buy-back options to get people into homes faster.

206998.explanation: Volt believes that these building regulations are important for an attractive living environment, as was demonstrated in Meerstad. These regulations help keep new neighbourhoods pleasant and visually appealing.

206999.explanation: Finding student housing shouldn’t be a lottery. Rooms are too few and too expensive. Together with housing corporations, schools, and the national government, Volt wants to build more affordable student housing and learn from countries with successful housing plans like Portugal and France.

207000.explanation: Volt wants to build 20,000 additional sustainable and affordable homes in 10 years. This may sometimes involve building on agricultural land.

207002.explanation: Volt looks to Vienna: The city builds affordable housing, not for profit. Through fair social rent, cooperatives, and resident input, housing stays fair and liveable. Volt wants to improve Groningen in the same way.

207003.explanation: Volt believes everyone should have the chance to participate through work, education, or other contributions. The municipality should focus less on control and more on support, for example when finding suitable employment.

207004.explanation: Volt believes motivation and the willingness to participate are central, as opposed to control. Education, work experience, and income support schemes help residents develop and find work, with support from social organizations.

207006.explanation: Volt wants people to be supported respectfully and without stigma in finding suitable work, while safeguarding their basic security. You should be able to refuse a job if it isn’t suitable to your training.

207007.explanation: Volt supports extra income assistance and minimum-income policies for things like bikes, devices, or school costs, so residents can develop themselves without financial worries getting in the way.

207008.explanation: Volt chooses a trust-based approach. By simplifying rules and reducing bureaucracy, problems are solved while social cohesion is maintained.

207009.explanation: Volt wants to prioritize safe, liveable neighbourhoods with play and sports areas. Streets where cars are “guests” improve safety, climate resilience, and the neighbourhood’s appeal.

207010.explanation: Volt does not explicitly aim to reduce parking space availability, but focuses on liveable neighbourhoods and alternative transport. We want to work with residents and businesses to find the best solution without widening the socio-economic gap with surrounding areas.

207011.explanation: Volt wants to keep parking fees affordable so the city remains accessible by car. Revenue from paid parking can be used for cheap and accessible public transport passes.

207012.explanation: Volt believes the city should be accessible to everyone. Walkable streets are more important than car parking spaces. We will install new bicycle platforms, improve public transport, and make cycling and walking routes safer.

207013.explanation: Volt wants stricter environmental rules than the national minimum. Eliminating environmental zones doesn’t fit this approach. We tackle pollution and noise and strengthen health measures in vulnerable neighbourhoods.

207014.explanation: Volt wants to make roads in Groningen safer by adjusting speed limits to match the road design within built-up areas. This prevents accidents and ensures traffic is safer, more pleasant, and greener.

207015.explanation: Volt believes sex workers should receive broad support. Voluntary programs are good, but those who want to continue in the profession should also have access to care and help, for example against exclusion and discrimination based on their work.

207016.explanation: Volt wants to promote green living environments, for example with community gardens, but not every neighbourhood needs them. Volt believes it’s important that residents decide for themselves how to green their area, for example through Neighbourhood Budgets.

206949.explanation: Last year, thanks in part to GroenLinks-PvdA, a citizens' assembly was organised on the subject of waste. This was a success. A citizens' assembly helps you make good choices, everyone is heard, and it increases trust in democracy.

206950.explanation: The parliamentary inquiry shows that gas was more important than the people of Groningen. This really needs to change. A single municipal team will enable the people of Groningen to deal with earthquake damage more quickly, better and more effectively, and houses can be reinforced better and faster.

206951.explanation: We choose to invest. We use the proceeds from property tax to pay for things that are important to our residents. We build cycle paths and ensure safe routes to school. We provide more housing, arrange healthcare and create many sports and play areas and cultural facilities.

206952.explanation: For many, their student years are the best time of their lives, but the housing shortage and pressure to perform can also make it difficult. With a student affairs department, we can help the 40,000 students in Groningen even better, especially if students also work there as experts by experience.

206953.explanation: We are working together towards a sustainable future. We do this by focusing more on the reuse of materials. We also purchase locally and sustainably. The municipality sets a good example. This also applies to our social goals.

206954.explanation: Heritage and local traditions are important, but culture is more than that. It connects Groningen's past with innovation and outside influences. Thanks to subsidies for museums, pop venues, amateur art and theatres, we want culture to be accessible to everyone. Including people with limited means.

206955.explanation: Young people in Groningen can count on the municipality when they need help. We should therefore not cut back, but rather invest in reducing waiting lists. By intervening early, a lot of suffering can be prevented. We want to give social workers more freedom and reduce their administrative burden.

206956.explanation: A ban on begging does not solve poverty. It does not help to fine people in vulnerable positions. We are committed to providing help, shelter and guidance. We opt for an approach involving experts by experience rather than a ban on begging.

206957.explanation: We invest in both climate and care. Good climate policy is also social policy. In Groningen, we are opting for a fair energy transition: CO₂-neutral by 2035 and with support for people with less money to make their homes more sustainable and reduce their energy bills. We’re not cutting back on that.

206958.explanation: The phasing out of fossil fuels is necessary for a sustainable Groningen. In our tenders and procurement, we therefore encourage companies to use as little fossil fuels as possible. Where possible, we only do business with companies that do not use fossil fuels.

206959.explanation: Sitting at an outdoor café or terrace is fun and creates a pleasant vibe. But, money is also earned from outdoor cafés or terraces on municipal land. We charge a fee for this. In addition to these places, we also provide enough free seating areas where you can sit without having to buy anything.

206960.explanation: Advertising tax reduces clutter in public spaces by reducing advertising or by charging a fair, small fee for it. Currently, most advertisements and businesses are exempt, and there are additional exemptions for social organisations.

206961.explanation: We invest more in neighbourhoods with many children living in poverty, because sadly, where you are born still determines your future. We make Groningen fairer through good education, after-school activities and meeting places. A Groningen with the very best community centres and green spaces.
