206962.explanation: Groningen's wonderful nightlife has no closing times, and as far as GroenLinks-PvdA is concerned, that will remain the case. We also want to extend Sunday opening hours. However, we must protect small Groningen entrepreneurs without staff who cannot always be open.

206963.explanation: Groningen helps people fleeing war and violence. There is no asylum crisis, but a shelter crisis due to lengthy procedures and a shortage of housing. We opt for a humane policy with temporary shelter, so that asylum seekers can stay here safely and are not left on the streets.

206964.explanation: All people in Groningen in urgent situations should be given priority for social housing, such as after a divorce, an unsafe home situation or other causes. Whether you are a refugee or not. People who are allowed to stay here should be able to start their lives in the Netherlands.

206965.explanation: We want to ensure that no one sleeps on the streets in Groningen. And in Groningen no one is illegal. That is why we offer help to people in vulnerable positions. People who live on the streets do not have the opportunity to think about the future or work towards returning home.

206966.explanation: Employers must provide decent housing for migrant workers, but it need not be at or near the workplace. Otherwise, job loss could lead to losing your home immediately. Many migrant workers are at risk, which we address through the Property Brigade (pandbrigade).

206967.explanation: Everyone should be able to participate in the municipality of Groningen, even if you have little money. It is also important for status holders and refugees to be able to participate in sports, cultural and children's activities with their families.

206968.explanation: We aim for a sustainable Groningen and work with the many companies that want to establish themselves here. Only businesses that operate sustainably may settle here. We set requirements for CO2 emissions, reuse of raw materials, and the sustainable use of personnel among others.

206969.explanation: We want to improve the welfare of fish by providing better information about animal-friendly fishing. At the same time, Groningen's anglers enjoy nature, relax and socialise with friends while fishing. We want to preserve this, but in the most animal-friendly way possible.

206970.explanation: To combat climate change, we need to use less energy. Burning fossil fuels to heat terraces consumes a lot of energy. By establishing regulations as a municipality, we ensure that businesses have clarity and switch to alternatives.

206971.explanation: Mowing less is good for nature and insects. This way, we preserve nature, protect biodiversity and create greener neighbourhoods and villages.

206972.explanation: In Groningen, we know why you shouldn’t rely on Groningen (or Russian) gas. We must phase out gas and may need to require people to switch.However, this will be done very carefully and with enough subsidies and support for people who cannot afford it themselves.

206973.explanation: Because Groningen runs its own municipal heating company, rates are far lower than elsewhere in the Netherlands. We can also give social housing residents discounts and we can choose which neighbourhoods the heating network will be expanded to. Selling it would be a huge waste!

206974.explanation: We want the municipality to be in charge of the building of solar parks and to use the profits to help people with little money to become more sustainable. Space between the panels allows greenery where insects and other animals feel at home. This enhances biodiversity, especially on farmland.

206975.explanation: We are creating 'Het Groningse Bos' (The Groningen Forest): at least 60 hectares of new forest in new locations or near existing forests. With more trees, we strengthen biodiversity, create more green spaces to enjoy and combat climate change.

206976.explanation: We are proud of the lively municipality of Groningen. Events after 11 p.m. are part of that. Groningen is a municipality with many different residents and wishes. We are committed to striking a balance between the desire for liveliness and tranquillity, small and large, and with an eye for nature.

206977.explanation: Sustainable events are necessary for the sustainable Groningen we are working towards. When granting permits, we set sustainability requirements wherever possible. We are also investing heavily in the racecourse as an event location, as the greenest festival location in the Netherlands.

206978.explanation: Groningen has a fantastic nightlife, and everyone should feel safe there. Through a pilot project, we want to investigate whether a safe space can contribute to the safety of women and queer people in particular in nightlife. In this way, we ensure a safe nightlife for everyone.

206979.explanation: We are proud of the many festivals in Groningen. We strive for a balance between small and large festivals, liveliness and tranquillity, and respect for nature. We also focus on small festivals that are free to attend, so that festivals are accessible to everyone.

206980.explanation: Groningen has a wonderful nightlife. This will only remain so if everyone feels safe and risks are reduced. By testing party drugs, we make nightlife more enjoyable and safer. Testing also provides an opportunity to discuss the risks of drug use.

206981.explanation: Sport should be accessible to everyone, and money should not be a barrier to exercise. By not making it free for everyone, we make it financially possible to make it free for parents and children with little money. This also includes money for sports clothing and sports shoes.

206982.explanation: Groningen played an important role in the slave trade as a trading city. We want to acknowledge this past and its impact on the present, for example by naming new streets after unsung heroes from our shared past. Such as Tulaplein.

206983.explanation: Together, we commemorate the history of slavery, but we also reflect on its impact on the present. We do this together with minority communities by creating two monuments, making Keti Koti a local holiday and structurally safeguarding Bitterzoet Erfgoed (Bittersweet heritage).

206984.explanation: Carbid shooting and Easter fires are important traditions with a rich history. They are part of village life and bring people together. Of course, it is important to ensure the safety of people and animals.

206986.explanation: Art and culture connect people and offer interaction and entertainment. All children in Groningen should experience this. We´re expanding the Culture Hopper and the Youth Fund for Sport & Culture. Through cultural education, ensuring every child encounters art and culture early.

206987.explanation: There are no commercial investors now, and we want to keep it that way. FC Groningen belongs to FC Groningen. If new ownership is needed, we would choose supporters over investors. The club’s identity and long-term policy come before quick profits.

206988.explanation: A major pop venue near the station will attract big acts and strengthen Groningen’s cultural scene. With the cultural sector, residents and entrepreneurs, we’ll build a venue to be just as proud of as the Forum. We’re also working on improving the Oosterpoort.

206989.explanation: BOAs do important work to keep our municipality safe. Unfortunately, aggression against BOAs is on the rise. A baton will not prevent this. It may even contribute to further escalation and does not make our BOAs any safer. The national government should invest more in the police instead of batons.

206990.explanation: Everyone should be able to be themselves in Groningen. The municipality leads by example among its staff. We trust in the neutrality of our special investigating officers, regardless of how they express their faith. Neutrality is something you do and achieve through good education and training.

206991.explanation: Safety matters and requires effective enforcement through good cooperation between BOAs, the police and, for example, security personnel. We invest broadly in safety, including prevention and care solutions. Simply increasing the number of special investigating officers is not the solution.

206992.explanation: We are proud of Groningen's nightlife. Everyone should be able to enjoy it safely. We invest in safety by focusing on equal opportunities, care, prevention and also repression. Temporary CCTV surveillance as a final measure, together with other measures, is also part of this.

206993.explanation: People from inside and outside Groningen can be valuable to our municipality. In housing development, priority can be given to people with local ties, and this should be taken into account, particularly in the villages in Groningen.

206994.explanation: We need nurses and police officers, but we also need IT specialists and road builders, for example. It is therefore impossible to choose between people on the basis of their profession; above all, we need to build faster, more and more affordably so that there is housing for everyone.

206995.explanation: In area developments like Stadshavens and Suikerzijde, buildings may exceed six floors. We are in favour of good architecture and a vibrant municipality where people can live comfortably and affordably. Tall buildings deserve good design and should fit in well with their surroundings.

206996.explanation: Homes are for living in, not for making money.

206997.explanation: We are in favour of more homes in Groningen. Given the current housing crisis, we do not accept that owners leave their properties vacant for unnecessarily long periods. By making them pay tax, we ensure that more homes and properties become available to live in.

206998.explanation: Aesthetic regulations are agreements that ensure that houses look good and provide clarity. They create a pleasant living environment and do not slow down housing construction.

206999.explanation: Groningen is the most beautiful student city in the Netherlands. Every year, thousands of students choose Groningen as their new home. We are proud and happy about this. That is why we have decided to invest in good and fair housing for students.

207000.explanation: Groningen is a compact, lively city with short travel distances and good facilities. In the coming decades we’ll build new neighbourhoods like Suikerzijde and Meerstad to provide more and sufficient housing. We will not expand further on agricultural land at the expense of the Groningen landscape.

207002.explanation: We are in favour of neighbourhoods and districts with affordable owner-occupied and social housing. All neighbourhoods and villages must have room for the poor, the rich, the young and the elderly. That is fair and ensures a pleasant society for everyone.

207003.explanation: Everyone should be able to count on a sufficient and predictable income through work or benefits. Security of livelihood is a right. It is therefore appropriate that social assistance policy is based on trust and what is possible. Compulsory reciprocal obligations are not in line with this.

207004.explanation: Everyone in Groningen should be able to participate, regardless of whether they have a diploma or not, or whether they can find a job easily or not. Basic jobs are a good and proven effective way to help people who have difficulty finding work. That is why we are in favour of more basic jobs.

207006.explanation: Everyone should be able to count on a secure and predictable income through work or benefits. Financial security is a right. That is why social assistance policy must be based on trust and what is possible. Coercion does not help to get everyone involved.

207007.explanation: The costs of school, bicycles and laptops are unaffordable for some families. We want children from families with less money to be able to participate just like everyone else. That is why we think it is fair for the local authority to pay for this.

207008.explanation: The costs of school, bicycles and laptops are unaffordable for some families. We want children from families with less money to be able to participate just like everyone else. That is why we think it is fair for the local authority to pay for this.

207009.explanation: In many streets, everything revolves around the car. We can counter this by making the car a guest. Cars must then adapt to children playing and pedestrians. Cars are welcome, but life on the street is more important.

207010.explanation: The space on the street belongs to everyone. Parking takes up a lot of space, leaving less room for children, bicycles and greenery. Fewer parking spaces and more space for playing, cycling and trees will create a pleasant municipality for everyone.

207011.explanation: In Groningen we pay a fair price for car parking, which helps fund bicycle parking, among other things. We will keep it that way. We separate a resident’s first car, visitors' cars and other people's cars. We make exceptions for informal carers and social organisations.

207012.explanation: There is a lot to do in our municipality and little space. That is why you have to choose how you use the space. Car parking spaces take up a lot of public space. We want fewer parking spaces and more space for playing, meeting, nature and cycling.

207013.explanation: Car emissions cause air pollution. The environmental zone ensures that fewer commercial vehicles drive in the city centre. This makes the air in the city cleaner and healthier. Clean air is vital for people.

207014.explanation: Car emissions cause air pollution. The environmental zone ensures that fewer commercial vehicles drive in the city centre. This makes the air in the city cleaner and healthier. Clean air is vital for people.

207015.explanation: Sex work is work. Groningen helps anyone seeking a new job so we also help sex workers. If sex workers are being exploited, it is important that the municipality helps them escape this situation by, among other things, providing accessible programmes with assistance.

207016.explanation: Sex work is work. Groningen helps anyone seeking a new job so we also help sex workers. If sex workers are being exploited, it is important that the municipality helps them escape this situation by, among other things, providing accessible programmes with assistance.

206949.explanation: A citizens’ assembly helps make decisions people recognise, because residents chosen by lot have a real say. That can build trust.

206950.explanation: People now get lost in a bureaucratic maze. We want one team and one contact point for damage, reinforcement and compensation: personal, clear, and only done when it’s truly solved.

206951.explanation: When we can, we lower property tax. But national government keeps cutting municipalities, so that space often isn’t there. We prioritise protecting services and shielding low incomes from local taxes.

206952.explanation: Students deserve good support, but not a separate department. We want clear, well-functioning services for everyone: less bureaucracy, more solutions—for students and other Groningers.

206953.explanation: Buying circular and sustainable saves money, makes products last longer, and keeps jobs and know-how local. Public money goes further and supports businesses that treat people and materials fairly.

206954.explanation: Heritage and local traditions deserve support, but not at the expense of other culture. We choose a broad cultural policy with room for neighbourhood culture, amateur arts and young makers.

206955.explanation: Too many children and parents wait too long for help. Cuts make problems bigger and heavier. We invest so support is nearby, available faster, and families are not left on their own.

206956.explanation: A begging ban solves nothing. It pushes people with debts or without a roof further into trouble. You don’t fight poverty with fines, but with help, shelter and a real future.

206957.explanation: You shouldn’t pit care against climate. Good climate policy lowers energy bills and is anti-poverty policy. But if national cuts force choices, we will always protect care. People come before projects.

206958.explanation: An absolute ban is unworkable: the city could hardly do business with anyone. It would hit small entrepreneurs without alternatives. We do break with big fossil multinationals and invest in public, clean energy. Tackle the polluter, not the market vendor.

206959.explanation: Terraces use public space to earn money, so a fair fee is logical. It helps maintain public space and prevents costs being shifted to residents who don’t profit from it.

206960.explanation: We spare small businesses and social organisations. We do tax large advertising from big companies—they profit from our public space and should contribute fairly. That keeps the city pleasant and shares the burden fairly.

206961.explanation: Some neighbourhoods have had too little for years. It’s only fair to invest extra there now. That way everyone in Groningen can live decently—in every neighbourhood.

206962.explanation: Unlimited opening hours favour big chains with lots of staff. Small shops get pressured to keep up. Clear rules protect local businesses that keep neighbourhoods and villages lively.

206963.explanation: Emergency shelter isn’t ideal, but no one should sleep on the street. Shelter must be shared fairly, also in wealthier areas. National government must deliver structural solutions. If needed, we take

206964.explanation: Everyone who urgently needs a home should be treated fairly. Refugees with a residence permit should get the same urgency as others who need housing fast. Extra priority is not fair to other people waiting.

206965.explanation: Stopping shelter for people without a valid permit helps no one. They may not stay but cannot leave yet. Without shelter they disappear onto the streets, causing more unrest and nuisance. Bed-bath-bread keeps them in view and under supervision.

206966.explanation: Living with your boss makes workers dependent and too often leads to abuse. We demand decent housing and enforce strictly, so labour migrants live safely and neighbourhoods don’t pay the price.

206967.explanation: The Stadjerspas is for residents on a low income, not for specific groups. Refugees can get it once they live here and meet the income rules. That keeps it fair, simple and equal for everyone.

206968.explanation: For us, sustainability is more than a green label: it also means fair pay, secure contracts and good employment. New companies settling in Groningen must meet those standards. That’s how we build a clean and fair economy.

206969.explanation: Angling is an affordable and relaxing hobby for many people. It belongs to our city and villages. We see no reason to ban it.

206970.explanation: A total ban isn’t needed. Patio heating should not waste energy, so we set clear standards. Sustainable options should be allowed, so entrepreneurs can green their business and people can still sit comfortably outside.

206971.explanation: Good maintenance matters. Where it looks messy or becomes unsafe, we mow more often—smart and ecological: tidy where it must be, room for nature where it can. So every neighbourhood stays cared for, safe and pleasant.

206972.explanation: No one will be forced to quit gas overnight. We believe in a voluntary switch with good support. New-builds are already gas-free by national rules. For existing homes: affordable, step by step, with residents.

206973.explanation: Keeping Warmtestad public lets the city steer for affordable tariffs instead of profit. We want heat to become cheaper and fairer for Groningers, not more expensive.

206974.explanation: We don’t rule out solar fields on farmland, but only if done carefully, publicly owned and in consultation with neighbours. The benefits must flow back to Groningers, not to investors.

206975.explanation: More forest and green space means cooling, cleaner air and nice places to relax. We invest in new woodland where Groningers truly benefit, with attention to nature and accessibility.

206976.explanation: Events add to the city, but people must also be able to live and sleep. Clear limits on times and noise protect residents and keep Groningen liveable, while leaving room for fun.

206977.explanation: Events should be organised sustainably—especially big festivals with a large impact. For small neighbourhood activities the rules should be simple and doable. We green events step by step without blocking volunteers and local initiatives.

206978.explanation: Going out should be safe for everyone. A safe space gives people a place to get immediate help after sexual violence or boundary-crossing behaviour. It provides quick support and makes nightlife areas safer.

206979.explanation: Festivals belong in the city, but not at the expense of liveability. It’s not about fewer festivals as such, but less nuisance: clear limits on noise and noisy days, and fair spreading across places and times.

206980.explanation: Drug testing prevents accidents and saves lives. Nightlife areas need accessible places where people can test safely. That’s not encouragement, but sensible public health policy that reduces harm.

206981.explanation: Every child should be able to play sports, regardless of parents’ income. Sport boosts health, confidence and having fun together. That’s why we ensure fees are never a barrier for children.

206982.explanation: It’s good to make resistance heroes from the slavery past more visible in our city. But we don’t want a fixed obligation. Naming should leave room for different stories from Groningen’s and our shared history.

206983.explanation: The slavery past deserves a shared commemoration. Organising it with community organisations creates recognition, involvement and a ceremony that connects and is carried by the city.

206984.explanation: Carbide shooting and Easter bonfires are local traditions that bring people together. We keep them with clear agreements on safety, environment and nuisance so everyone can enjoy them.

206986.explanation: Every child should experience art and culture, regardless of income at home. It boosts creativity, confidence and joy. Investing in this gives children more chances to develop.

206987.explanation: We’ve seen at clubs like Vitesse how risky dependence on commercial investors can be. With supporters owning half, FC Groningen stays in Groningers’ hands. A football club is not a toy for investors.

206988.explanation: We support a good pop venue near the station, but ‘big’ isn’t a goal in itself. It must fit demand and stay within budget. No prestige project, but a sober, smart investment that gives Groningen a strong pop stage.

206989.explanation: BOAs are not the police. Arming them raises the risk of escalation. We improve safety by investing in neighbourhoods: more community and youth workers and strong cooperation with local police. Prevent problems instead of letting them escalate.

206990.explanation: A uniform is meant to be neutral and recognisable. BOAs exercise public authority and must be clearly identifiable as such. That’s why we follow BOA professionals: one uniform, without personal expressions.

206991.explanation: In many neighbourhoods and nightlife areas enforcement is lacking. More BOAs mean visibility, e.g. against dumped rubbish, safer nightlife and less drug-related nuisance. Enforcement should support liveable neighbourhoods, not unnecessary repression.

206992.explanation: CCTV can help in some situations, but it isn’t a solution by itself. Without enforcement and prevention, problems just move elsewhere. We use cameras only targeted and temporary, as part of a broader safety approach.

206993.explanation: New-build housing should first benefit people who live, work or study in Groningen. A local-connection rule helps keep homes from ending up with investors and keeps neighbourhoods liveable for Groningers.

206994.explanation: Teachers, nurses and police officers are essential for our city. If high housing costs or part-time work block an affordable home, we should ensure they can still live here. That keeps Groningen running.

206995.explanation: To tackle the housing shortage we must build, including upwards. But not randomly. High-rise fits in some places and not in others. We decide per neighbourhood what makes sense, so new homes match the area.

206996.explanation: Homes are for living, not for profit. Investors and slumlords drive up prices and make housing unaffordable. An owner-occupancy requirement keeps homes for people who want to live here.

206997.explanation: Leaving homes empty while people can’t find housing is unacceptable. A vacancy levy pushes owners to use buildings for living again. Vacancy must not be a business model: the longer it stays empty, the heavier the consequences.

206998.explanation: Without design rules, building quickly becomes a mess. Clear standards ensure new homes fit the neighbourhood and keep Groningen beautiful and liveable.

206999.explanation: Landlords charge high rents and let buildings and neighbourhoods deteriorate. That harms students and the living environment. We support housing associations to build affordable, good student homes, so students can live decently without burdening neighbourhoods.

207000.explanation: In villages the housing shortage is severe. Sometimes building on farmland is needed so people can stay in their own village. We do it carefully, respecting the landscape and the village’s character.

207002.explanation: Affordable rent should be possible in every neighbourhood and village. Social housing shouldn’t be concentrated in just a few areas. We build where it’s missing to break the divide and keep Groningen one municipality.

207003.explanation: If someone does socially useful work, they deserve proper pay. Mandatory ‘return’ work isn’t a solution but disguised forced labour. We want real jobs with prospects, not unpaid work.

207004.explanation: Basic jobs give people for whom a regular job isn’t straightforward meaningful, paid work. They offer structure, dignity and work that matters in neighbourhoods and villages. That’s why we expand to 1,000 basic jobs.

207006.explanation: Cutting benefits doesn’t help anyone forward. Sometimes work isn’t possible temporarily due to health, caring duties or other issues. Then people need support and stability, not punishment. Cuts push people deeper into trouble.

207007.explanation: Every child should be able to take part at school, even if there’s little money at home. We reimburse bikes, laptops and other necessary school costs so children aren’t sidelined by poverty.

207008.explanation: For other types of fraud there are no separate click-lines. A special reporting tool stigmatizes people on benefits and fuels mistrust. We choose equality and trust, with normal checks like for everyone else.

207009.explanation: In some streets cars should be ‘guests’, giving more space to pedestrians, cyclists and children at play. That makes neighbourhoods safer and more liveable. We do it where it fits, together with residents.

207010.explanation: In many areas parking is already difficult. Simply removing spaces increases pressure and just moves the problem. We keep parking workable and decide per neighbourhood what’s needed so people can park near home.

207011.explanation: Parking fees are too high for many residents and squeeze monthly budgets. Parking must not be a cash cow. We want lower and fairer fees so residents and their visitors can park affordably near home.

207012.explanation: More bike parking is welcome, but not at the expense of car parking. The city centre must remain accessible for everyone, including people who depend on a car. We choose balance, not exclusion.

207013.explanation: Low-emission zones must not exclude people or small entrepreneurs. The current zone rightly has many exemptions. We want tailored rules and generous waivers so greening stays fair and affordable for everyone.

207014.explanation: 30 km/h makes streets safer and calmer, but only if roads are designed for it, with safe crossings and less rat-running. We set 30 km/h as the norm, except on the ring road and main routes.

207015.explanation: Anyone who wants to leave sex work should get good support. With guidance, care and help into other work we improve safety and give people perspective. That’s social policy without judgement.

207016.explanation: Food gardens can mean a lot for meeting each other and sustainability. Where residents want this, the city should provide space and support. But not every neighbourhood or village is suitable. We choose tailoring, not an obligation.

206949.explanation: The democratic rule of law is the foundation of our society. As DURF, we would like to see permanent neighborhood councils that not only consult but also decide. Not only politicians should decide the municipality’s course; citizens must also have this right.

206950.explanation: Less paperwork, fewer rules, the citizen’s interest at the center – that is our commitment. If successful, we are in favor. That team must be given the decisiveness to tackle this; otherwise, it is just another counter.

206951.explanation: The price increases of houses and thus also the property tax are already high enough. Therefore, this tax on home ownership must be reduced.

206952.explanation: Students deserve attention; the municipal council represents them. But for DURF, this does not require yet another municipal department – DURF chooses freedom and lower costs. Also for students.

206953.explanation: Who pays for this? You do! So the municipality must purchase products and services smartly and cheaply. Local and sustainable? That’s fine but only if it costs nothing extra.

206954.explanation: DURF stands up for local interests. That is why DURF believes cultural subsidies should go to local traditions and heritage.

206955.explanation: The municipality should focus on simplification and cutting the large bureaucracy, not on the care itself. Care must always be available for citizens – and that can be done well without extra money.

206956.explanation: Livable and safe Groningen: no begging scene, but real help and shelter.

206957.explanation: Stop the wasteful climate policy that makes citizens poorer. Focus on real local needs like affordable care, or better yet: give the money back to Groningers through tax reductions.

206958.explanation: That makes no sense and is impossible. Almost half of the construction costs of the Forum were actually paid by NAM. Gasunie is one of the largest employers. UMCG is one of the city’s largest users of sour gas.

206959.explanation: DURF wants fewer taxes and more freedom. Therefore, we are against terrace taxes.

206960.explanation: DURF wants less tax and more freedom for citizens and entrepreneurs. Therefore, we oppose extra taxes on billboards, facade advertising, and illuminated advertisements.

206961.explanation: DURF wants to improve the livability of all neighborhoods, not just the poorer ones. But often these issues are most urgent in poorer neighborhoods.

206962.explanation: DURF supports freedom. Therefore, entrepreneurs should be able to determine their own opening hours.

206963.explanation: We do not give someone from another province priority over others. So why prioritize someone from another country? The municipality should focus on local housing problems. One effect of this policy is a pull factor.

206964.explanation: We believe asylum seekers are given too much priority over the interests of Groningers. If Groningers cannot find affordable housing and struggle to pay groceries, it cannot be explained that people from other countries are provided for.

206965.explanation: Interests of Groningers first – build affordable housing for locals. By the time we have solved this, there may be room for others.

206966.explanation: For DURF, it is not the municipality’s task to pay for housing for foreign workers. Local workers often cannot find affordable accommodation. Employers should solve this themselves.

206967.explanation: DURF supports equality. We are therefore against discrimination based on background. The Stadjerspas is for the poorest Groningers, regardless of whether they are refugees or not.

206968.explanation: DURF stands for freedom. Companies are welcome, providing jobs and services. Sustainability is a vague term. Companies must comply with legally established emission and pollution standards.

206969.explanation: DURF stands for freedom; practicing angling is part of this.

206970.explanation: DURF is against coercion and for freedom. If it is affordable for an entrepreneur to make the outdoors pleasant for guests without disturbing others, this is not a municipal matter. Moreover, this is simply a consequence of earlier policy: the indoor smoking ban.

206971.explanation: The municipality is responsible for proper maintenance of public spaces, also to improve traffic safety (visibility). As long as this remains, we have no preference for more or less frequent mowing.

206972.explanation: At DURF we are always against coercion, pressure, or other forms of influence on the freedom of choice. People who want to heat their homes with gas should be able to do so just as much as those who want to heat their homes with a heat pump.

206973.explanation: DURF stands for lower taxes and maximum freedom: companies that sustain themselves through voluntary contributions? At DURF, that always takes priority over subsidies from our wallets.

206974.explanation: It is absurd to sacrifice our beautiful fertile Groningen farmland for solar panels; food security is more important than a few meager kilowatts of electricity! Solar panels on roofs are fine, if the owner wants it. There is plenty of space available this way without having to use farmland.

206975.explanation: Let's first make room for our real problems. Space for housing, space for Groningen residents. DURF does not want to expropriate farmers. So how do you want to realize this? We prefer better maintenance and expansion of existing green spaces and parks.

206976.explanation: DURF supports festivals: they keep the city lively and interesting. The organizer should be able to continue longer, but in consultation with neighbors. Compensation for neighbors might be a solution.

206977.explanation: The municipality should facilitate business activity and events. This only leads to unnecessary regulatory burdens and costs for organizing a festival.

206978.explanation: The entire nightlife area must be safe. The owners of hospitality venues have this responsibility, and the police can assist them. Public safety is the police's task.

206979.explanation: If people want to organize or celebrate something, the municipality should facilitate that. A municipality should always encourage business activity and events because that keeps Groningen vibrant.

206980.explanation: No tax money for drug testing; entrepreneurs in the nightlife area can handle this perfectly well.

206981.explanation: Taxes must be lowered enough that people can always afford sports club memberships for their children.

206982.explanation: Proud of our culture and history: let's name new streets after icons from Groningen and the Netherlands.

206983.explanation: Let citizens commemorate or honor whoever they want themselves; this is not a matter for the municipality. We prefer to focus on combating modern slavery.

206984.explanation: Cultural expressions and traditions must be preserved. The municipality's role is to facilitate these, not to prohibit them.

206986.explanation: We already pay for art and culture. Art and culture should come from the people themselves. The municipality can simply require museums and cultural institutions to make art and culture accessible to youth in exchange for the existing subsidies.

206987.explanation: Groningen has a long-term interest in the club as cultural heritage. It is important that the municipality does not become financially involved. The municipality can help supporters with expertise and know-how to initiate this.

206988.explanation: Why should the taxpayer foot the bill for something with a limited target group? There are plenty of commercial parties who would want to build this, too. The only thing the municipality should do is make space for these kinds of initiatives.

206989.explanation: This is what the police are trained for. We cannot lower the bar for the monopoly on violence; that is simply too impactful.

206990.explanation: The government must always be neutral. We are against religious or cultural expressions by (law enforcement) officials.

206991.explanation: Doubling the number of BOAs would also mean doubling the costs. Additional taxes would need to be levied to finance this. DURF is not in favor. It is better to deploy BOAs more selectively to improve livability than to hire more.

206992.explanation: We support privacy and see camera surveillance as an infringement on personal privacy. It also undermines the idea that you are innocent until proven guilty. With camera surveillance, everyone is potentially suspicious. These systems can also be abused, including data leaks.

206993.explanation: How do you determine a connection? This sounds vague and arbitrary. Those who have been on the list longest have the most right to a home.

206994.explanation: This undermines the principle of equality. No one has more rights than others. It is also arbitrary; why should these people take precedence over others? Don't cleaners, mechanics, and garbage collectors have just as much right to housing?

206995.explanation: Densifying first: the smartest and fastest way to address the housing shortage; building within the existing city!

206996.explanation: Freedom over rules: banning buying houses for children or family? Or for renting out? What does this actually solve? Moreover, you would need to set up an organization to check people's homes. We consider that undesirable.

206997.explanation: Freedom first: such rules don't work; investors circumvent them with anti-squatting contracts, only the small private individual suffers.

206998.explanation: Yes, under conditions: relaxing building regulations to reduce red tape; building remains high-quality and fits our streetscape. Especially small renovations should be possible without approval.

206999.explanation: We want this for everyone because everyone has the right to housing. Housing is a basic need.

207000.explanation: Focus on densification within the built-up area. New construction should minimize impact on nature, but housing is an acute problem, so we agree.

207002.explanation: But this is already largely the case in Groningen city.

207003.explanation: Everyone has control over their own body and time. This is the definition of exploitation. We are firmly against coercion.

207004.explanation: The municipality may facilitate economic activity but does not have the mandate to create jobs and subsidize them with public money.

207006.explanation: DURF supports freedom: occupational choice must be free. The municipality should not interfere with career choices.

207007.explanation: We want tax reductions so that everyone can simply afford these kinds of things.

207008.explanation: Public funds must not be misused; that is why we are in favor.

207009.explanation: There are already more than enough restrictions on car traffic. Don't make driving in Groningen impossible.

207010.explanation: Groningen is a growing city; behind the fish market the parking garage was closed by the municipality. We are now actually lacking parking spaces!

207011.explanation: These rates must be substantially lowered. The current rates are unjustifiable.

207012.explanation: Groningen is a growing city; there must be more parking spaces for both cars and bicycles!

207013.explanation: Away with this policy: it only leads to more patronizing, higher costs for citizens and entrepreneurs, enforcement costs, fines, and administrative hassle.

207014.explanation: There are already more than enough restrictions on car traffic. Don't make driving in Groningen impossible.

207015.explanation: Why are we spending money on this? Are there no bigger problems?

207016.explanation: We are in favor, as long as the initiative comes from the citizens themselves. The municipality's role is only to facilitate when there is demand for it. Bureaucracy and costs for the community must be limited to an absolute minimum.

206949.explanation: Instead of making plans for residents, we want to make plans with them. Citizens councils, including smaller-scale ones (such as neighborhood or village councils), play a prominent role in this. It's important that underrepresented groups in society have a voice in these discussions.

206950.explanation: Gas extraction has caused a disaster in Groningen. Many residents are still waiting for safe homes, resolved damage claims, and genuine recognition. As a government, we must take every step that contributes to damage recovery. Municipal assistance makes it more accessible.

206951.explanation: It's fair that homeowners pay property tax. Those who are financially stronger, should pay more than those in need. Property tax is a vital source of income for the municipality, funding amenities such as culture, green spaces, the fire department, education and public maintenance.

206952.explanation: Students are exploited by slum landlords and, due to performance pressure, are more likely to experience mental health issues during their studies. A student help desk can help young people with questions about rental agreements, their rights, and where to go for help with mental health issues.

206953.explanation: As a municipality, we must do everything we can to combat the climate and environmental crisis. We want Groningen to become the greenest municipality in the Netherlands, and circular, sustainable, and local purchasing are crucial for this.

206954.explanation: Art and culture is allowed to be uncomfortable, can change your perspective, and should be diverse. Subsidies should therefore also go to small-scale creators, not just heritage. We want to increase the funding for art and culture.

206955.explanation: Every young person has the right to good care. Promoting equal opportunities for everyone is necessary in prevention. We want to reduce waiting lists and solve bottlenecks in mental healthcare.

206956.explanation: It's a human right to ask others for help when circumstances prevent you from supporting yourself. We shouldn't criminalize vulnerable people, but rather, as a municipality, provide care. We only want to take action in cases of criminal exploitation of beggars.

206957.explanation: We want to invest in both the climate and healthcare. The last four years we showed that that’s possible. For example, by maintaining the property tax, we can invest in both.

206958.explanation: Everything indicates we've already surpassed the 1.5 degrees of global warming. We can turn the tide if we make different choices. We want to cut all ties with fossil fuel companies and are working on a ban on fossil fuel advertising.

206959.explanation: This tax is relatively low, and we use it to create a pleasant and green living environment. This is a major benefit for businesses and residents.

206960.explanation: Advertising encourages overconsumption. We want to ban advertising in public spaces, with the exception of government campaigns, cultural events, and charities. Until then, advertising will remain taxed.

206961.explanation: Pleasant housing is a human right. Poor neighborhoods deserve extra attention and investments. Wealthier neighborhoods are often greener with more facilities. By investing extra we ensure equal opportunities for everyone.

206962.explanation: Businesses should have the freedom to open and close whenever they want. We combat overconsumption, for example, by limiting advertising.

206963.explanation: Refugees are welcome! We provide proper shelter for everyone, especially childeren, and maintain the “bed-bath-bread arrangement”. We do not participate in immigration policies that violate human rights. We have special attention for vulnerable groups (such as LGBTQIA+).

206964.explanation: Everyone in a vulnerable position has priority access to rental housing, including refugees. If the national law against priority for refugees is passed, Groningen will instead create a local priority system. A safe roof over your head is a human right, regardless where you were born.

206965.explanation: No human being is illegal! Everyone has the right to humane shelter, regardless of residency status or papers. We maintain the bed-bath-bread arrangement.

206966.explanation: Companies in the Netherlands make millions by exploiting migrant workers. If they lose their jobs, they often lose their homes as well. By separating their housing and work, they are better protected. Municipalities must protect vulnerable groups from criminal landlords and employers.

206967.explanation: Everyone has the right to a decent life, regardless of their origin or residence status. A Stadjerspas gives them access to things like sports, culture, and veterinary care. This not only helps prevent poverty but also ensures they can participate in Groningen from day one.

206968.explanation: We want Groningen to become the greenest city in the Netherlands. We make critical choices about which businesses and sectors are allowed to establish themselves here. No polluting sources, such as livestock farms or harmful industries. The municipality encourages and supports sustainable startups.

206969.explanation: Fish feel pain. Therefore, angling is animal cruelty. We are working hard for a ban on angling. Until a ban on angling is in place, we advocate for a ban on barbs, fishing weights, and live nets. The welfare of animals always comes first and is more important than entertainment.

206970.explanation: Groningen is tackling energy waste together with local businesses. For example light pollution, terrace heating, and open shopdoors. Sustainable alternatives are being promoted.

206971.explanation: Flowers, insects, and habitats for animals should all have a place in our municipality. Mowing less helps in the nature crisis. In our mowing policy we mow in stages and under no circumstances during the breeding, mating, and nursing periods or during the animals' winter rest.

206972.explanation: There is no time to waste in fighting global warming. The energy transition must, however, be carried out fairly. Residents who have to switch to a district heating system will be compensated if their contract turns out to be more expensive.

206973.explanation: We want to have full control over ''Warmtestad''. This way, we can decide what happens with the revenues from those connections and ensure that the income flows back to our residents. In this way we invest in the heating network, sustainable heat sources, and local neighborhood heating.

206974.explanation: Agricultural land is primarily used for plant-based food and nature. If livestock farming decreases significantly, there will also be room for housingprojects. Solar panels are preferably installed on roofs and in exceptional cases we choose solar fields. No solar parks can be built in nature.

206975.explanation: To tackle the nature crisis and preserve habitats for animals living in the wild: forests are needed. In addition trees prevent overheated cities and enhance biodiversity. The more trees, the better. The ''MEER bos'' is a wonderful project by residents that we want to bring to life.

206976.explanation: No disruptive activities, such as events, are planned in or near nature reserves. For each type of event outside natural areas, the effects on local residents must be considered. A livable Groningen for both people and animals is important to our considerations.

206977.explanation: Events should be sustainable: free of waste, fireworks, polluting diesel generators and free of gas-powered heating columns. We do not want events that include animals. Subsidies are only granted to events that meet sustainability criteria.

206978.explanation: Everyone should be able to go out safely. Sexual violence and boundary-crossing behaviour are unacceptable. With 'safe spaces' we can create a place where people can turn to in case they feel unsafe. We want extra attention for the safety of women, racism, and people from the LGBTQIA+ community.

206979.explanation: Festivals are valuable cultural events, but we have to consider the impact on residents, animals, nature and the environment. Nature reserves and some parks are not suitable locations. The impact on the environment is an important consideration for the amount of festivals we should allow.

206980.explanation: We are expanding accessible drug testing to ensure safe drug use. At parties where hard drugs will likely be used we will deploy staff to provide information and, where necessary, support.

206981.explanation: We want all children to have access to sports and grow up healthy. Free membership can make the decision to participate in sports much easier, especially for children from low-income families. We do believe that parents with higher incomes can contribute to their children's sports clubs.

206982.explanation: Groningen's history of slavery and colonialism has left deep scars which consequences are still felt today. Street names in Groningen should reflect the history of all its residents. Diversity is the guiding principle.

206983.explanation: Groningen has to support and facilitate important commemorations and holidays for various residents, such as Eid al-Fitr, Yom Kippur, and Keti Koti. Groningen's slavery and colonial past has left deep scars, the consequences of which are still felt today.

206984.explanation: Easter bonfires cause a tremendous amount of air pollution that drifts into nature. In addition birds often build nests in the bonfires making bonfires a danger to animals. Loud bangs like fireworks and carbide cause significant anxiety in both pets and wild animals.

206986.explanation: Arts and culture are very important in any democracy. They encourage reflection, stir emotions, move and disrupt. Children must be able to develop their social-, emotional-, motor- and creative skills. Culture contributes to those things.

206987.explanation: FC Groningen is very important to many residents in Groningen. We want to support this process, but a substantial financial contribution is not feasible. The cost-benefit analysis, along with the contributions and vision of supporters and the club will be decisive factors in the decision.

206988.explanation: We have the best cultural offering in the north of the Netherlands. By investing in the renovation of De Oosterpoort and the construction of De Grote Popzaal, we are ensuring high-quality culture in the city for future generations.

206989.explanation: Violence isn't the solution. Furthermore institutional racism and ethnic profiling are a problem within the police and law enforcement. Groningen residents with a migration background may face disproportionate violence more often if enforcement officers are armed.

206990.explanation: Groningen has to follow the advice of the European Court of Human Rights. Therefore officers are permitted to wear religious symbols, such as a headscarf. This gives more people the opportunity to serve as enforcement officers.

206991.explanation: We want a government that protects citizens. The municipality will ensure sufficient enforcement officers who collectively represent society. Doubling the number of officers is not necessary. Additional deployment, for example, by the green enforcement officer, is.

206992.explanation: For privacy reasons, CCTV in public spaces is used very sparingly. It offers only a false sense of security and often simply displaces the problem. Automatic facial recognition is too drastic and should not be used.

206993.explanation: Housing must be distributed fairly. Everyone should have equal opportunities. In villages it can be helpful to give young people the option to stay in their village. Housing associations in cities already have a system of appropriate allocation.

206994.explanation: There's a shortage of social housing. Everyone who is entitled to social housing should have equal opportunities. That's what the Party for the Animals stands for. We do, however, support giving priority to people in a vulnerable situation.

206995.explanation: High-rise buildings are one solution for building homes, but also for implementing greener living spaces. High-rise buildings should be concentrated near amenities like supermarkets and larger public transport facilities. Quality of life must not be compromised.

206996.explanation: Housing is a right, and should not be a profit model. We will maintain the owner-occupancy requirement and combat vacancy. We believe the municipality should immediately implement anti-speculation measures wherever possible to keep investors out.

206997.explanation: The Party for the Animals advocates for measures to combat housing speculation and vacancy, such as a mandatory owner-occupancy requirement and a vacancy ordinance with penalties. A tax could also be effective. Long-term vacant properties should be made habitable.

206998.explanation: We want to build homes for the coming decades, not just the next few years. Building regulations help prevent poor-quality homes from being built or prevent the built of homes that don't fit well into their surroundings. Homes must be sustainable, healthy, and attractive.

206999.explanation: We support suitable housing for everyone. Students should be able to live in affordable housing. Young people should find a rental and first-home-buyers should find a home that suits their situation. Seniors should be able to move into accessible senior housing.

207000.explanation: Agricultural land is primarily intended for healthy food production and nature development. If livestock farming shrinks, there will be room for housing. However, we primarily focus on development in cities and villages by building nature-inclusive structures on the outskirts of neighborhoods.

207002.explanation: Social housing should be distributed across all neighborhoods and villages. We strive for a community where people connect, regardless of income. This must be done in a nature-inclusive way. Green spaces must be preserved.

207003.explanation: Groningen needs to shift its focus from making profit to ensure well-being. Social assistance is an important safety net and forcing others to do something in return actually increases inequality and stress. The municipality should primarily help people.

207004.explanation: The Party for the Animals believes no one deserves to be stressed about income. Basic jobs allow people to do meaningful work. The interests of those receiving benefits are very important and central in the situation.

207006.explanation: The Participation Act, with its strong emphasis on paid work and its mandatory nature, has failed. Groningen prioritizes residents and their needs and takes a broader and more generous view of welfare recipients. The municipality also recognizes unpaid work as participation.

207007.explanation: Equal opportunities starts with the children. That's why we're paying extra attention to children growing up in poverty or with language deficiencies. The municipality must ensure that every child can participate, regardless of their parents' income.

207008.explanation: As far as the Party for the Animals is concerned, there will be no hotline for benefit fraud in Groningen. The existing hotline will be shut down. Trust in our citizens must be paramount in the government. We don't want situations like the Dutch childcare benefits scandal.

207009.explanation: We discourage polluting traffic and we promote clean traffic. We give pedestrians and cyclists more space. We contribute to climate goals, reclaim green spaces in our neighbourhoods and make outdoor areas safer for children and animals.

207010.explanation: As far as we're concerned, no new parking spaces will be added. The municipality will eliminate some parking spaces and convert them into wider sidewalks, children's play areas, flower-filled verges, small parks, or bicycle parking spaces.

207011.explanation: Polluting cars should pay more, clean cars should pay less. Residents can park their cars in a nearby parking garage for a lower fee or park their cars cheaply in a P+R, where they can use free bicycles and affordable public transport. Parking permits and street parking should become more expensive.

207012.explanation: The municipality must eliminate parking spaces in some locations and convert them into wider sidewalks, children's play areas, flower-filled verges, small parks or bicycle parking spaces. Sufficient bicycle parking spaces will also be provided at major public transport stops and transfer points.

207013.explanation: Clean air is crucial for people, animals, and nature. Therefore, polluting traffic will be banned by expanding the low-emission zone to the entire built-up area. A zero-emission zone will remain in place for commercial traffic in the city center.

207014.explanation: Motorized traffic within built-up areas should have a speed limit of 30 km/h. Road layouts will be adapted where possible so we can create space for greenery and children can play in the streets.

207015.explanation: Sexwork policies should be developed with sex workers. Their voices are therefore paramount. Groningen guarantees safety for sex workers, while also respecting their self-determination. Providing support is our priority. We will continue the support- and exit program for sex workers.

207016.explanation: Every village, neighborhood, and district should have a food garden and a community center where people can meet and eat together. This way we bring people closer together, reduce health disparities and increase equal opportunities

statements.title: The municipality must reduce the number of parking spaces.
The municipality should not do business with companies that use fossil fuels.
The municipality must urge the national government to establish a single municipal team that manages everything relating to earthquake damage.
The majority of the subsidies for culture should go towards heritage and local traditions.

titleParts.glossary: Geld dat de overheid aan burgers, bedrijven en organisaties geeft om ze te steunen.

titleParts.text: for culture should go towards heritage and local traditions.

statements.title: All refugees in the municipality must receive a Stadjerspas. This is a pass specifically for people with little money.
The municipality must allow evening events to continue until later.
The municipality should spend the money for climate policy on care plans.
In nightlife and entertainment areas, there should be multiple places where people can have their drugs tested to ensure they are safe.
The municipality must help supporters of FC Groningen to become half-owners of the football club, instead of commercial investors.
The municipality must abolish the advertising tax. This includes taxes for facade advertisements, signs, illuminated advertising and the like.
The municipality must pay for a sports club membership for all children under the age of 16 years.
Teachers, nurses and police officers should be given priority for public-sector rented homes.
Employers must ensure that migrant workers live at or near their workplace.
Groningen must distribute public-sector rented homes throughout the municipality.
The municipality should establish a student affairs department that also employs students.
Groningen must sell the municipal heating company WarmteStad.
Municipal law enforcement officers (BOAs) may carry a baton.
The maximum speed should be 30 km/h everywhere, except on the ring road and a few main roads.
Half of the rental homes in new construction must go to people who have a connection to Groningen.
The municipality must have at least 60 hectares of new forest planted.
The municipality must abolish the rules concerning the external appearance of buildings for the construction and renovation of homes.
The municipality should have a large concert hall built on the south side of the station.
The option to report benefit fraud through the municipality's website should remain in place.
Anyone who buys a house in our municipality must live there themselves.
The municipality must reduce the tax on terraces.
The municipality must stop accommodating people who do not have a valid residence permit.
There should be more streets in which the priority of cars is reduced.
People who receive social assistance benefits must do socially meaningful work in return.
The municipality should spend more money on introducing children to art and culture.
Every village and every neighbourhood should have its own food garden.

shootoutStatements.title: In Groningen’s city centre, there should be more bicycle parking spaces and fewer car parking spaces.
For important issues, the municipality should establish a citizens’ assembly that advises the municipal council. The residents on a citizens’ assembly are selected by lottery.
The municipality should no longer provide emergency accommodation for asylum seekers. Emergency accommodation consists of temporary reception centres.
The municipality should primarily purchase circular and sustainable products and services.
Buildings of more than six storeys in height may be allowed in new construction in urban areas.
The municipality must allow carbide shooting in Ten Post and the Easter bonfires in Onnen and Noordlaren.
The municipality must invest more in the liveability of poorer neighbourhoods. This may be done at the expense of wealthier neighbourhoods.
The municipality should invest more money in student housing.
People on welfare who refuse a job offer should receive less money.
The municipality should ban fishing.
The municipality must allow new construction on agricultural land.
The number of municipal law enforcement officers (BOAs) must double.
The municipality should allow fewer festivals.
The property tax (OZB) must be reduced.
The municipality must not cut back on youth care.
The begging ban must remain in place.
The municipality must give refugees with a residence permit priority for a public-sector rented home.
Retailers and hospitality entrepreneurs should be able to determine their own opening hours.
Only businesses that operate sustainably may become established in the Municipality of Groningen.
The municipality should mow the verges and public grassed areas more often.
The municipality must ban terrace heating.
The municipality may not force anyone to stop using gas.
The municipality may only permit events that are organised in a sustainable manner.
There should be no solar farms on agricultural land.
In nightlife and entertainment areas, there should be a safe space where people can go in the event of sexual violence and inappropriate behaviour.
There needs to be more camera surveillance in the Groningen city centre.
The municipality should name new streets and squares after resistance heroes from slavery history.
Parking rates in the Municipality of Groningen must be reduced.
The municipality must join forces with social organisations to organise the annual commemoration of slavery history.
There should be no environmental zones in Groningen.
Municipal law enforcement officers (BOAs) must be able to wear a headscarf, kippah, or turban.
The municipality should provide more funding for programmes for sex workers who want to leave sex work.
Owners who leave their properties vacant for more than six months must pay tax to the municipality for this.
The municipality must provide more basic employment. Basic employment consists of subsidised jobs for people who do not have ready access to the labour market.
The municipality should reimburse bicycle, laptop and school costs for children from low-income families.
